title.zh: 卷九十五 西南夷兩粵朝鮮傳
title.en: Volume 95: Traditions of the Southwest Yi peoples, the two Yues, and Chosun (Korea)
title.raw: 卷九十五 西南夷兩粵朝鮮傳 Volume 95: Traditions of the Southwest Yi peoples, the two Yues, and Chosun (Korea)

sentences.zh: 南夷君長以十數，夜郎最大。

translations.literal: Among the southern Yi, the chiefs numbered in the tens, and Yelang was the greatest.
translations.idiomatic: South of the Han heartland, Yi chiefs ran into the dozens; Yelang was the largest of them.

sentences.zh: 其西，靡莫之屬以十數，滇最大。

translations.literal: To their west, those belonging to Mimo numbered in the tens, and Dian was the greatest.
translations.idiomatic: West of them, Mimo-affiliated groups likewise numbered in the dozens, and Dian dominated.

sentences.zh: 自滇以北，君長以十數，邛都最大。

translations.literal: North of Dian, the chiefs numbered in the tens, and Qiongdu was the greatest.
translations.idiomatic: North of Dian, chiefdoms again ran into the tens; Qiongdu was foremost.

sentences.zh: 此皆椎結，耕田，有邑聚。

translations.literal: All of these wore their hair in a topknot, cultivated fields, and had settlements.
translations.idiomatic: They all wore the topknot, farmed fixed fields, and lived in clustered towns.

sentences.zh: 其外，西自桐師以東，北至葉榆，名爲巂、昆明、編發，隨畜移徙，亡常處，亡君長，地方可數千里。

translations.literal: Beyond them, from west of Tongshi eastward and north to Yeyu, they were named Xi, Kunming, and plaited-hair peoples; they followed their herds and shifted camp, had no fixed abode and no chiefs, and their territory extended perhaps several thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: Outside that belt, from Tongshi east to Yeyu in the north, lay peoples known as Xi, Kunming, and the plait-haired tribes—pastoral nomads without permanent homes or titled chiefs, spread across thousands of li.

sentences.zh: 自巂以東北，君長以十數，徙、莋都最大。

translations.literal: Northeast from Xi country, the chiefs numbered in the tens, and the Xi tribal lands and Zuodu were the greatest.
translations.idiomatic: Northeast of Xi country, chiefdoms again ran into the tens; the Xi tribe’s sphere and Zuodu were the largest.

sentences.zh: 自莋以東北，君長以十數，冉駹最大。

translations.literal: Northeast from Zuo, the chiefs numbered in the tens, and Ranmang were the greatest.
translations.idiomatic: Northeast of Zuo, chiefdoms again numbered in the tens; Ranmang led them.

sentences.zh: 其俗，或土著，或移徙。

translations.literal: Among their customs, some were native sedentary peoples and some were migratory.
translations.idiomatic: Some groups stayed put; others moved with the seasons.

sentences.zh: 在蜀之西。

translations.literal: They lay west of Shu.
translations.idiomatic: All of this lay west of Shu.

sentences.zh: 自駹以東北，君長以十數，白馬最大，皆氐類也。

translations.literal: Northeast from Mang, the chiefs numbered in the tens, and Baima was the greatest; all were Di peoples.
translations.idiomatic: Northeast of Mang, chiefdoms again ran into the tens; Baima was paramount, and these were Di communities.

sentences.zh: 此皆巴、蜀西南外蠻夷也。

translations.literal: These were all the barbarian Yi beyond Ba and Shu to the southwest.
translations.idiomatic: They were the barbarian peoples beyond Ba and Shu to the southwest.

sentences.zh: 始楚威王時，使將軍莊蹻將兵循江上，略巴、黔中以西。

translations.literal: At the beginning, in the time of King Wei of Chu, he ordered General Zhuang Qiao to lead troops along the river upward and seize territory west of Ba and Qianzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In King Wei of Chu’s day, the court sent General Zhuang Qiao up the Yangzi to carve out ground west of Ba and Qianzhong.

sentences.zh: 莊蹻者，楚莊王苗裔也。

translations.literal: Zhuang Qiao was a descendant of King Zhuang of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuang Qiao traced his line to King Zhuang of Chu.

sentences.zh: 蹻至滇池，方三百里，旁平地肥饒數千里，以兵威定屬楚。

translations.literal: When Qiao reached Lake Dian, it measured three hundred li on a side; the surrounding flatlands were fertile for several thousand li, and by military might he fixed it as belonging to Chu.
translations.idiomatic: He reached Dian Lake—three hundred li across—with rich plains stretching for thousands of li beyond; force of arms brought the region under Chu’s shadow.

sentences.zh: 欲歸報，會秦擊奪楚巴、黔中郡，道塞不通，因乃以其衆王滇，變服，從其俗以長之。

translations.literal: Intending to return and report, it happened that Qin struck and seized Chu’s Ba and Qianzhong commanderies; the roads were blocked and impassable, and so with his host he became king in Dian, changed his dress, followed their customs, and ruled them.
translations.idiomatic: He meant to march home with word of conquest, but Qin snatched Ba and Qianzhong from Chu and sealed every route; stranded, he declared himself king in Dian, adopted local dress, and governed by local ways.

sentences.zh: 秦時嘗破，略通五尺道，諸此國頗置吏焉。

translations.literal: Under Qin they had once been broken, opened the Five-Foot Road in rough fashion, and in these states largely placed officials.
translations.idiomatic: The Qin had earlier battered the region open, hacked through the Five-Foot Road, and posted magistrates across many of these polities.

sentences.zh: 十余歲，秦滅。

translations.literal: After more than ten years, Qin perished.
translations.idiomatic: A dozen years later Qin fell.

sentences.zh: 及漢興，皆棄此國而關蜀故徼。

translations.literal: When Han rose, all abandoned these states and closed them off with Shu’s old frontier barriers.
translations.idiomatic: When Han arose, the court walked away from those dependencies and sealed them behind Shu’s former border posts.

sentences.zh: 巴、蜀民或竊出商賈，取其莋馬、僰僮、旄牛，以此巴、蜀殷富。

translations.literal: People of Ba and Shu sometimes slipped out to trade, obtaining their Zuo horses, Bo slaves, and yak-oxen; by this Ba and Shu grew rich.
translations.idiomatic: Ba and Shu commoners smuggled trade beyond the line for Zuo horses, Bo bondsmen, and hairy cattle—trade that fattened the Two Shu.

sentences.zh: 建元六年，大行王恢擊東粵，東粵殺王郢以報。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Jianyuan, Grand Coachman Wang Hui attacked Eastern Yue; Eastern Yue killed King Ying to answer him.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianyuan 6, Grand Coachman Wang Hui struck Eastern Yue; the Yue court executed King Ying to placate Han.

sentences.zh: 恢因兵威使番陽令唐蒙風曉南粵。

translations.literal: Hui, leveraging military prestige, sent Poyang Magistrate Tang Meng to sound out Southern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Riding that show of force, Hui dispatched Poyang’s magistrate Tang Meng to probe Southern Yue.

sentences.zh: 南粵食蒙蜀枸醬，蒙問所從來，曰：「道西北牂柯江，江廣數里，出番禺城下。

translations.literal: Southern Yue served Meng Shu dogwood sauce; Meng asked where it came from, and they said: ‘By way northwest along the Zangke River—the river is several li wide and empties below Panyu city.’
translations.idiomatic: Southern Yue treated Meng to Shu dogwood paste; when he asked its route, they said it came down the Zangke—several li across—under the walls of Panyu.

sentences.zh: 」蒙歸至長安，問蜀賈人，獨蜀出枸醬，多持竊出市夜郎。

translations.literal: ’ Meng returned to Chang’an and questioned Shu merchants; only Shu produced dogwood sauce, and many carried it out secretly to sell in Yelang.
translations.idiomatic: Back in Chang’an he cornered Shu traders: only Shu made that condiment, they said, and smugglers ran most of it to Yelang markets.

sentences.zh: 夜郎者，臨牂柯江，江廣百余步，足以行船。

translations.literal: As for Yelang, it bordered the Zangke River; the river was more than a hundred paces wide and sufficient for boats.
translations.idiomatic: Yelang sat on the Zangke, wide enough for more than a hundred paces—ample draft for shipping.

sentences.zh: 南粵以財物役屬夜郎，西至桐師，然亦不能臣使也。

translations.literal: Southern Yue by wealth brought Yelang into dependent service, west to Tongshi, yet could not truly subject and command them.
translations.idiomatic: Southern Yue bought Yelang’s loyalty as far west as Tongshi, but never reduced it to vassalage.

sentences.zh: 蒙乃上書說上曰：「南粵王黃屋左纛，地東西萬餘里，名爲外臣，實一州主。

translations.literal: Meng then submitted a memorial persuading the emperor, saying: ‘The King of Southern Yue rides the yellow canopy and left-banner pennons; his territory spans more than ten thousand li east to west. Though called an outer vassal, he is in truth lord of a province.’
translations.idiomatic: Tang Meng petitioned the throne: Southern Yue’s king sports an imperial yellow canopy and left pennant; his realm stretches ten thousand li east and west—outwardly a vassal, in practice a regional sovereign.

sentences.zh: 今以長沙、豫章往，水道多絕，難行。

translations.literal: Now if one goes from Changsha and Yuzhang, the waterways are largely broken off and hard to travel.
translations.idiomatic: Striking from Changsha or Yuzhang means broken water routes—an arduous approach.

sentences.zh: 竊聞夜郎所有精兵可得十萬，浮船牂柯，出不意，此制粵一奇也。

translations.literal: I have heard that Yelang’s picked troops may reach one hundred thousand; float boats on the Zangke and strike unexpectedly—this is one extraordinary means to control Yue.’
translations.idiomatic: Yelang fields perhaps a hundred thousand good troops; a fleet down the Zangke could take Yue by surprise—that is the flank worth buying.’

sentences.zh: 誠以漢之強，巴、蜀之饒，通夜郎道，爲置吏，甚易。

translations.literal: Truly, given Han’s strength and Ba–Shu’s abundance, opening a road to Yelang and placing officials there would be very easy.’
translations.idiomatic: With Han’s might and Shu’s wealth, a corridor into Yelang and county seats there would be straightforward.’

sentences.zh: 」上許之。

translations.literal: ’ The emperor approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃拜蒙以郎中將，將千人，食重萬餘人，從巴苻關入，遂見夜郎侯多同。

translations.literal: He then appointed Meng as Colonel of the Gentlemen of the Household, with a thousand troops and more than ten thousand porters and baggage handlers; entering through Ba’s Fuguan Pass, he thereupon met the Marquis of Yelang, Duotong.
translations.idiomatic: Meng was commissioned colonel of household troops—one thousand fighters and ten thousand support—entered via Ba’s Fuguan, and was received by Yelang’s chief Duotong.

sentences.zh: 厚賜，諭以威德，約爲置吏，使其子爲令。

translations.literal: He generously gifted him, instructed him in awe-inspiring virtue, agreed to place officials, and made his son a magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: Rich gifts, lectures on Han power and grace, a pact to post officials, and Duotong’s son named county magistrate.

sentences.zh: 夜郎旁小邑皆貪漢繒帛，以爲漢道險，終不能有也，乃且聽蒙約。

translations.literal: The small settlements beside Yelang all coveted Han silks, yet thought Han’s roads perilous and that ultimately Han could not hold them, and so for the moment listened to Meng’s agreement.
translations.idiomatic: Petty chiefdoms round Yelang craved Han brocade yet assumed the terrain would keep Han out for good; they accepted Meng’s terms for now.

sentences.zh: 還報，乃以爲犍爲郡。

translations.literal: On his return report, the region was made Qianwei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His report home yielded the creation of Qianwei commandery.

sentences.zh: 發巴、蜀卒治道，自僰道指牂柯江。

translations.literal: Ba and Shu corvée troops were mobilized to build a road from Bodao to the Zangke River.
translations.idiomatic: Ba and Shu draftees carved a highway from Bodao to the Zangke.

sentences.zh: 蜀人司馬相如亦言西夷邛、莋可置郡。

translations.literal: The Shu man Sima Xiangru also said the western Yi of Qiong and Zuo could have commanderies placed.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Xiangru of Shu likewise urged counties on the western Yi of Qiong and Zuo.

sentences.zh: 使相如以郎中將往諭，皆如南夷，爲置一都尉，十餘縣，屬蜀。

translations.literal: He sent Xiangru as Colonel of the Gentlemen of the Household to instruct them; all followed the southern Yi pattern—one commandant was placed, more than ten counties, subordinate to Shu.
translations.idiomatic: Xiangru went south as colonel with the same brief as the southern campaign: one commandant, a dozen-odd counties, all under Shu.

sentences.zh: 當是時，巴、蜀西郡通西南夷道，載轉相餉。

translations.literal: At this time Ba and Shu’s western commanderies opened roads to the southwestern Yi, and wagon relays supplied one another.
translations.idiomatic: Ba and Shu’s western counties fed supply trains into the Yi frontier.

sentences.zh: 數歲，道不通，士罷餓餧，離暑濕，死者甚衆。

translations.literal: For several years the road did not open; troops grew weary, hungry, and wasted away; suffering heat and damp, the dead were very many.
translations.idiomatic: Years passed without a usable road; soldiers starved and wilted in the malarial heat—casualties mounted.

sentences.zh: 西南夷又數反，發兵興擊，耗費亡功。

translations.literal: The southwestern Yi repeatedly rebelled; troops were raised to strike them, consuming resources without achievement.
translations.idiomatic: Repeated Yi revolts drew costly, fruitless expeditions.

sentences.zh: 上患之，使公孫弘往視問焉。

translations.literal: The emperor worried over it and sent Gongsun Hong to go inspect and inquire.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor dispatched Gongsun Hong to survey the mess.

sentences.zh: 還報，言其不便。

translations.literal: Returning with his report, he said it was not advantageous.
translations.idiomatic: Hong reported that the venture did not pay.

sentences.zh: 及弘爲御史大夫，時方築朔方，據河逐胡，弘等因言西南夷爲害，可且罷，專力事匈奴。

translations.literal: When Hong became imperial counselor, the court was then building Shuofang, holding the Yellow River and driving off the Hu; Hong and others therefore said the southwestern Yi were harmful and could for the moment be abandoned, concentrating effort on the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Once Hong rose to imperial counselor—just as Shuofang rose on the northern frontier—he argued the Yi drain should wait while Han focused on the steppe.

sentences.zh: 上許之，罷西夷，獨置南夷兩縣一都尉，稍令犍爲自保就。

translations.literal: The emperor approved it, abandoned the western Yi, and solely retained two southern Yi counties and one commandant, gradually ordering Qianwei to secure itself as it could.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor concurred: western counties were folded back, only two southern Yi counties and a commandant remained, and Qianwei was told to hold what it could.

sentences.zh: 及元狩元年，博望侯張騫言使大夏時，見蜀布、邛竹杖，問所從來，曰：「從東南身毒國，可數千里，得蜀賈人市。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanshou, Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian reported that when he had been sent to Bactria he saw Shu cloth and Qiong bamboo staffs; he asked where they came from, and they said: ‘From Shendu country to the southeast, perhaps several thousand li away—obtained from Shu merchants in trade.’
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshou 1, Zhang Qian told the throne how in Bactria he had spotted Shu cloth and Qiong bamboo staffs; asked their origin, locals traced them to Shendu—thousands of li southeast—via Shu traders.

sentences.zh: 」或聞邛西可二千里有身毒國。

translations.literal: ’ Some heard that west of Qiong, perhaps two thousand li away, lay Shendu country.
translations.idiomatic: Others claimed Shendu lay some two thousand li west of Qiong.

sentences.zh: 騫因盛言大夏在漢西南，慕中國，患匈奴隔其道，誠通蜀，身毒國道便近，又亡害。

translations.literal: Qian therefore strongly argued that Bactria lay southwest of Han, admired the Central States, and worried that the Xiongnu blocked its route; truly if Shu were opened, the road through Shendu would be convenient and close, and without harm.
translations.idiomatic: Qian pressed the point: Bactria sits southwest of Han, looks toward China, and chafes at Xiongnu obstruction—a Shu corridor through Shendu would be shorter and safer.

sentences.zh: 於是天子乃令王然子、柏始昌、呂越人等十餘輩間出西南夷，指求身毒國。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven ordered Wang Ranzi, Bo Shichang, Lü Yueren, and more than ten parties to slip out by separate routes through the southwestern Yi, aiming to seek Shendu country.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor dispatched Wang Ranzi, Bo Shichang, Lü Yueren, and a dozen others into the Yi hills by stealth, hunting a route to Shendu.

sentences.zh: 至滇，滇王當羌乃留爲求道。

translations.literal: Reaching Dian, King Dangqiang of Dian detained them to seek a road.
translations.idiomatic: At Dian, King Dangqiang held the envoys while he looked for a passage.

sentences.zh: 四歲餘，皆閉昆明，莫能通。

translations.literal: After more than four years, all were blocked at Kunming and could not get through.
translations.idiomatic: Four years on, Kunming tribes bottled every party—no breakthrough.

sentences.zh: 滇王與漢使言：「漢孰與我大？

translations.literal: The King of Dian said to Han envoys: ‘Is Han or I greater?’
translations.idiomatic: The Dian king asked the Han envoys: ‘Which is larger—Han or my realm?’

sentences.zh: 」及夜郎侯亦然，各自以一州王，不知漢廣大。

translations.literal: ’ And the Marquis of Yelang was likewise; each reckoned himself king of one province and did not know Han’s vastness.
translations.idiomatic: Yelang’s chief asked the same—each man imagined himself lord of a world and had no notion of Han’s scale.

sentences.zh: 使者還，因盛言滇大國，足事親附。

translations.literal: When the envoys returned, they accordingly strongly declared Dian a great state, sufficient to serve by drawing close.
translations.idiomatic: Back at court they painted Dian as a prize ally worth cultivating.

sentences.zh: 天子注意焉。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven fixed his attention on it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took notice.

sentences.zh: 及至南粵反，上使馳義侯因犍爲發南夷兵。

translations.literal: When Southern Yue rebelled, the emperor ordered Marquis of Chiyi to mobilize southern Yi troops through Qianwei.
translations.idiomatic: Southern Yue’s revolt brought orders for the Marquis of Chiyi to levy Yi auxiliaries out of Qianwei.

sentences.zh: 且蘭君恐遠行，旁國虜其老弱，乃與其衆反，殺使者及犍爲太守。

translations.literal: The lord of Qielan feared a distant march; neighboring states might seize his elderly and weak, and so with his host he rebelled, killing envoys and the grand administrator of Qianwei.
translations.idiomatic: Qielan’s chief refused the long march lest neighbors loot his rear; he turned his men on Han, slaughtering the envoys and Qianwei’s governor.

sentences.zh: 漢乃發巴、蜀罪人當擊南粵者八校尉擊之。

translations.literal: Han therefore dispatched eight colonels of Ba and Shu convicts slated to strike Southern Yue to attack them.
translations.idiomatic: Han redirected eight colonels of Ba–Shu penal troops bound for Yue against the rebels.

sentences.zh: 會越已破，漢八校尉不下，中郎將郭昌、衛廣引兵還，行誅隔滇道者且蘭，斬首數萬，遂平南夷爲牂柯郡。

translations.literal: It happened that Yue had already been broken; Han’s eight colonels therefore did not march south (to Yue), but Colonel of the Gentlemen Guo Chang and Wei Guang led troops back, executing along the way Qielan, which blocked the road to Dian, beheading several tens of thousands, and thereupon pacified the southern Yi as Zangke commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Yue collapsed before those eight wings reached it; Guo Chang and Wei Guang turned their columns west, crushed Qielan—the chokepoint on the Dian road—claimed tens of thousands of heads, and folded the south into Zangke commandery.

sentences.zh: 夜郎侯始倚南粵，南粵已滅，還誅反者，夜郎遂入朝，上以爲夜郎王。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Yelang had relied on Southern Yue; Southern Yue was already destroyed, and returning they executed the rebels, whereupon Yelang entered court audience, and the emperor made him King of Yelang.
translations.idiomatic: Yelang had leaned on Yue; with Yue gone and rebels punished, its chief came to court and left ennobled as king.

sentences.zh: 南粵破後，及漢誅且蘭、邛君，並殺莋侯，冉駹皆震恐，請臣置吏，以邛都爲粵巂郡，作都爲沈黎郡，冉駹爲文山郡，廣漢西白馬爲武都郡。

translations.literal: After Southern Yue was broken, and Han executed Qielan and the lord of Qiong, and simultaneously killed the marquis of Zuo, Ranmang all trembled in fear, requested to submit as subjects and have officials placed; Qiongdu was made Yuexi commandery, Zuodu was made Shenli commandery, Ranmang was made Wenshan commandery, and Baima west of Guanghan was made Wudu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Yue’s collapse and Han’s killing of Qielan, the Qiong lord, and the Zuo marquis terrified Ranmang—the lot sued for county rule: Qiongdu became Yuexi, Zuodu Shenli, Ranmang Wenshan, and Guanghan’s western Baima lands Wudu.

sentences.zh: 使王然於以粵破及誅南夷兵威風諭滇王入朝。

translations.literal: He sent Wang Ranyu to instruct the King of Dian to enter court using the prestige of Yue’s defeat and the execution of southern Yi forces.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Ranyu carried word of Yue’s ruin and southern heads on stakes to coax Dian’s king to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 滇王者，其衆數萬人，其旁東北勞深、靡莫皆同姓相杖，未肯聽。

translations.literal: The King of Dian’s hosts numbered in the tens of thousands; to his northeast Laoshen and Mimo, all of the same surname supporting one another, were unwilling to listen.
translations.idiomatic: Dian fielded tens of thousands; allied Laoshen and Mimo kinsmen on his northeast flank refused the summons.

sentences.zh: 勞、莫數侵犯使者吏卒。

translations.literal: Lao and Mo repeatedly violated and attacked envoys, clerks, and soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Those allies repeatedly ambushed Han messengers and escorts.

sentences.zh: 元封二年，天子發巴、蜀兵擊滅勞深、靡莫，以兵臨滇。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanfeng, the Son of Heaven mobilized Ba and Shu troops, attacked and destroyed Laoshen and Mimo, and brought troops to bear on Dian.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanfeng 2 the emperor sent Ba–Shu armies to wipe out Laoshen and Mimo, then massed on Dian’s border.

sentences.zh: 滇王始首善，以故弗誅。

translations.literal: The King of Dian had been first to do good, and for this reason was not executed.
translations.idiomatic: Dian’s king had stayed comparatively loyal, so his life was spared.

sentences.zh: 滇王離西夷，滇舉國降，請置吏入朝，於是以爲益州郡，賜滇王王印，復長其民。

translations.literal: The King of Dian left the western Yi; Dian’s whole state surrendered, requesting officials be placed and to enter court; thereupon it was made Yizhou commandery, the King of Dian was granted a king’s seal, and again ruled his people.
translations.idiomatic: Dian quit the western league and surrendered wholesale, asking for magistrates and tributary status—Han mapped it as Yizhou, handed the king a royal seal, and let him govern his own folk under Han law.

sentences.zh: 西南夷君長以百數，獨夜郎、滇受王印。

translations.literal: Southwestern Yi chiefs numbered in the hundreds; only Yelang and Dian received king seals.
translations.idiomatic: Scores of Yi chiefs existed; only Yelang and Dian won kingly chops.

sentences.zh: 滇，小邑也，最寵焉。

translations.literal: Dian was a small polity, yet was most favored.
translations.idiomatic: Tiny Dian drew the court’s fondest treatment.

sentences.zh: 後二十三歲，孝昭始元元年，益州廉頭、姑繒民反，殺長吏。

translations.literal: After twenty-three years, in the first year of Emperor Zhao’s Shiyuan era, the people of Liantou and Guzeng in Yizhou rebelled and killed senior officials.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-three years on, in Emperor Zhao’s first Shiyuan year, Liantou and Guzeng in Yizhou rose and slew their magistrates.

sentences.zh: 牂柯、談指、同並等二十四邑，凡三萬餘人皆反。

translations.literal: Twenty-four settlements such as Zangke, Tanzhi, and Tongbing, in all more than thirty thousand people, all rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-four polities—Zangke, Tanzhi, Tongbing, and the rest—put more than thirty thousand rebels in the field.

sentences.zh: 遣水衡都尉發蜀郡、犍爲奔命萬餘人擊牂柯，大破之。

translations.literal: He dispatched the superintendent of the imperial household to mobilize more than ten thousand emergency-response troops from Shu commandery and Qianwei to strike Zangke and greatly defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: The court ordered the palace superintendent to scrape together ten thousand crack troops from Shu and Qianwei; they shattered the Zangke rebels.

sentences.zh: 後三歲，姑繒、葉榆復反，遣水衡都尉呂辟胡將郡兵擊之。

translations.literal: After three years Guzeng and Yeyu rebelled again; he dispatched the superintendent of the imperial household Lü Pihu to lead commandery troops against them.
translations.idiomatic: Three years later Guzeng and Yeyu rose again; Lü Pihu led county levies against them.

sentences.zh: 辟胡不進，蠻夷遂殺益州太守，乘勝與辟胡戰，士戰及溺死者四千餘人。

translations.literal: Pihu did not advance; the barbarian Yi thereupon killed the grand administrator of Yizhou, and pressing their victory fought Pihu; soldiers killed in battle and drowned numbered more than four thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Pihu stalled; the rebels slew Yizhou’s governor, then routed his hesitant column—four thousand dead by steel or river.

sentences.zh: 明年，復遣軍正王平與大鴻臚田廣明等並進，大破益州，斬首捕虜五萬餘級，獲畜產十余萬。

translations.literal: The next year he again dispatched Army Rectifier Wang Ping and Grand Herald Tian Guangming and others to advance together; they greatly defeated Yizhou, beheading and capturing more than fifty thousand heads, and seized livestock numbering more than one hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Wang Ping and Grand Herald Tian Guangming drove a coordinated offensive, shattered Yizhou resistance—fifty thousand counted heads—and drove off a hundred thousand head of stock.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「鉤町侯亡波率其邑君長人民擊反者，斬首捕虜有功，其立亡波爲鉤町王。

translations.literal: The emperor said: ‘Marquis of Gouding Wang Bo led his settlement chiefs, elders, and people to strike the rebels; in beheading and capturing he had merit—establish Wang Bo as King of Gouding.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ruled: ‘Gouding marquis Wang Bo rallied his yeomen against the rebels and earned blood merit—elevate him to king of Gouding.’

sentences.zh: 大鴻臚廣明賜爵關內侯，食邑三百戶。

translations.literal: Grand Herald Guangming was granted rank as marquis within the passes, with an estate of three hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Herald Tian Guangming took a secondary marquisate and three hundred taxable households.

sentences.zh: 」後間歲，武都氐人反，遣執金吾馬適建、龍額侯韓增與大鴻臚廣明將兵擊之。

translations.literal: ’ After a year’s interval the Di people of Wudu rebelled; he dispatched Bearer of the Mace Mashijian, Marquis of Long’e Han Zeng, and Grand Herald Guangming to lead troops against them.
translations.idiomatic: ’ A year later Wudu’s Di rose; Mashijian, Han Zeng of Long’e, and Tian Guangming marched to suppress them.

sentences.zh: 至城帝河平中，夜郎王興與鉤町王禹、漏臥侯俞更舉兵相攻。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Cheng’s Heping era, the King of Yelang Xing and the King of Gouding Yu and Marquis of Louwo Yu repeatedly raised troops and attacked one another.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng, in the Heping years, Yelang’s king Xing, Gouding’s king Yu, and Louwo marquis Yu traded blows in endless rounds.

sentences.zh: 牂柯太守請發兵誅興等，議者以爲道遠不可擊，乃遣太中大夫蜀郡張匡持節和解。

translations.literal: The grand administrator of Zangke requested troops be raised to execute Xing and others; deliberators deemed the roads too distant to strike, and so they dispatched Grandee of the Palace Zhang Kuang of Shu commandery bearing credentials to reconcile them.
translations.idiomatic: Zangke’s governor wanted Xing’s head; court opinion cited distance and vetoed force—instead Zhang Kuang of Shu went south with an ivory tally to mediate.

sentences.zh: 興等不從命，刻木象漢吏，立道旁射之。

translations.literal: Xing and the others did not obey the command; they carved wood to resemble Han officials, set them by the road, and shot them.
translations.idiomatic: The kings ignored the order, whittled Han-style figurines, lined the highway with them, and used them for archery practice.

sentences.zh: 杜欽說大將軍王鳳曰：「太中大夫匡使和解蠻夷王侯，王侯受詔，已復相攻，輕易漢使，不憚國威，其效可見。

translations.literal: Du Qin persuaded Grand General Wang Feng, saying: ‘Grandee Zhang Kuang was sent to reconcile the barbarian kings; the kings received the edict yet already attacked each other again, treated Han envoys lightly, and did not fear state majesty—the lesson is plain to see.’
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin warned Wang Feng: Zhang Kuang’s mission bought only a pause—the kings took the edict and kept fighting, mocking Han envoys and daring state prestige—the insult speaks for itself.’

sentences.zh: 恐議者選耎，復守和解，太守察動靜有變，乃以聞。

translations.literal: I fear deliberators will choose weakness and again cling to reconciliation; the grand administrator observes until there is change in the situation and only then reports.
translations.idiomatic: Weak ministers will cling to talk while the frontier governor waits for smoke on the horizon before cabling the throne.’

sentences.zh: 如此，則復曠一時，王侯得收獵其衆，申固其謀，黨助衆多，各不勝忿，必相殄滅。

translations.literal: If so, then again a season is wasted; the kings get to gather and hunt their followers, reinforce their plots, factions and helpers become numerous, each unable to contain rage—they will surely mutual annihilate.
translations.idiomatic: Months slip away; each ruler rallies kin, hardens conspiracy, stacks allies, rage boils—and they will tear one another apart.’

sentences.zh: 自知罪成，狂犯守尉，遠臧溫暑毒草之地，雖有孫、吳將，賁、育士，若入水火，往必焦設，知勇亡所施。

translations.literal: Knowing their guilt complete, they madly assault guardians and commandants, fleeing far to stash themselves in hot damp lands of poisonous grass—even with Sun Bin and Wu Qi generals, Ben and Yu warriors, it is like entering fire or water; going there they will surely be burnt and destroyed, wit and courage have nowhere to apply.
translations.idiomatic: Once guilt sets in they will strike county seats and bolt into fever-country—even Sun Wu tactics and elite infantry burn up like kindling; wit and valor vanish in that jungle.’

sentences.zh: 屯田守之，費不可勝量。

translations.literal: To garrison-farm and hold them—the expense cannot be reckoned.
translations.idiomatic: Trying to hold it by frontier farms would bleed the treasury beyond counting.’

sentences.zh: 宜因其罪惡未成，未疑漢家加誅，陰敕旁郡守尉練士馬，大司農豫調穀積要害處，選任職太守往，以秋涼時入，誅其王侯尤不軌者。

translations.literal: It is fitting to take advantage while their crimes are not yet formed and they do not suspect Han will add execution—secretly order neighboring guardians and commandants to drill troops and horses, have the minister of finance beforehand transfer grain and stockpile at chokepoints, select a capable grand administrator to go, enter in the cool of autumn, and execute those kings most unruly.
translations.idiomatic: Better strike before guilt ripens into suspicion—quiet orders to drill neighboring commands, grain dumps on the passes, a seasoned governor, a dry-season thrust, and decapitate the worst offenders.’

sentences.zh: 即以爲不毛之地，亡用之民，聖王不以勞中國，宜罷郡，放棄其民，絕其王侯勿復通。

translations.literal: If instead one supposes it barren land and useless people that a sage king does not weary the Central States over, then it is fitting to abolish the commandery, abandon the people, cut off the kings and have no further communication.
translations.idiomatic: If you judge it sterile soil and worthless subjects no sage king should tax China for—then strike the commandery from the map, walk away, and seal the passes.’

sentences.zh: 如以先帝所立累世之功不可墮壞，亦宜因其萌牙，早斷絕之，及已成形然後戰師，則萬姓被害。」

translations.literal: If because former emperors established merit accumulated across generations it cannot be ruined, then likewise it is fitting to seize them at the sprout and cut them off early; wait until fully formed then wage armies and the myriad surnames suffer harm.’
translations.idiomatic: If you cherish the edifice earlier emperors raised—then nip this at the bud; wait until armies must march against finished rebellion and common folk pay the price.’

sentences.zh: 大將軍鳳於是薦金城司馬陳立爲牂柯太守。

translations.literal: Grand General Feng thereupon recommended Chen Li, marshal of Jincheng, as grand administrator of Zangke.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng nominated Jincheng marshal Chen Li for Zangke.

sentences.zh: 立者，臨邛人，前爲連然長，不韋令，蠻夷畏這。

translations.literal: Chen Li was a man of Linqiong; formerly he had been chief of Lianran and magistrate of Buwei; the barbarian Yi feared him.
translations.idiomatic: A Linqiong native, he had run Lianran and Buwei—Yi leaders learned to dread him.

sentences.zh: 及至牂柯，諭告夜郎王興，興不從命，立請誅之。

translations.literal: When he reached Zangke, he instructed and announced to the King of Yelang Xing; Xing did not obey the command, and Li requested to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: At his post he summoned Yelang’s king Xing; defiance brought Chen Li’s memorial demanding execution.

sentences.zh: 未報，乃從吏數十人出行縣，至興國且同亭，召興。

translations.literal: Before a reply came, he then went out inspecting counties with several tens of clerks, reaching Qietong Pavilion in Xing’s domain, and summoned Xing.
translations.idiomatic: Before capital approval arrived he toured with a light escort to Qietong Pavilion in Yelang and called Xing out.

sentences.zh: 興將數千人往至亭，從邑君數十人入見立。

translations.literal: Xing led several thousand men who arrived at the pavilion; several tens of settlement lords followed him in to see Li.
translations.idiomatic: Xing marched thousands to the pavilion; dozens of village headmen followed him inside.

sentences.zh: 立數責，因斷頭。

translations.literal: Li repeatedly rebuked him and thereupon cut off his head.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Li lectured him once, then took his head.

sentences.zh: 邑君曰：「將軍誅亡狀，爲民除害，願出曉士衆。

translations.literal: The settlement lords said: ‘The general executed him for his lawless misconduct and removed a scourge from the people; we wish to go out and explain this to the host.’
translations.idiomatic: The headmen said: ‘You executed a king who had forfeited all warrant of rule and rid us of a tyrant—let us walk out and calm the host.’

sentences.zh: 」以興頭示之，皆釋兵降。

translations.literal: ’ Showing them Xing’s head, all laid down weapons and surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Li lifted Xing’s head; every spear hit the ground.

sentences.zh: 鉤町王禹、漏臥侯俞震恐，入粟千斛，牛、羊勞吏士。

translations.literal: The King of Gouding Yu and Marquis of Louwo Yu trembled in fear; they contributed grain to the amount of a thousand hu and cattle and sheep to reward clerks and soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: Gouding’s Yu and Louwo’s marquis panicked—each sent a thousand hu of grain plus cattle and sheep for Han troops.

sentences.zh: 立還歸郡，興妻父翁指與興子邪務收餘兵，迫脅旁二十二邑反。

translations.literal: Li returned to the commandery; Xing’s father-in-law Wuzhi and Xing’s son Xiewu gathered remnant troops, coerced the neighboring twenty-two settlements, and rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Back at headquarters, Xing’s father-in-law Wuzhi and son Xiewu scraped together survivors and dragooned twenty-two neighboring towns into revolt.

sentences.zh: 至冬，立奏募諸夷與都尉長史分將攻翁指等。

translations.literal: By winter Li memorialized to recruit various Yi together with the commandant and chief clerk to divide command and attack Wuzhi and the others.
translations.idiomatic: That winter Chen Li asked to levy Yi auxiliaries under the commandant and chief clerk for a pincer on Wuzhi.

sentences.zh: 翁指據厄爲壘，立使奇兵絕其餉道，縱反間以誘其衆。

translations.literal: Wuzhi held the narrows and made a fortress; Li dispatched unusual troops to cut off their supply route and spread counter-intelligence to entice his masses.
translations.idiomatic: Wuzhi walled up the defile; Chen Li sent raiders to sever his trains and fed his ranks rumor.

sentences.zh: 都尉萬年曰：「兵久不決，費不可共。

translations.literal: Commandant Wannian said: ‘If troops delay long without a decision, the expense cannot be borne.’
translations.idiomatic: The commandant Wannian protested: ‘A drawn-out standoff will bleed the treasury dry.’

sentences.zh: 」引兵獨進，敗走，趨立營。

translations.literal: ’ He led troops forward alone, was defeated and fled, and raced toward Li’s encampment.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He charged ahead on his own, broke, and bolted for Chen Li’s lines.

sentences.zh: 立怒，叱戲下令格之。

translations.literal: He grew angry, rebuked him sharply, and ordered him struck down.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Li roared at the rout and ordered Wannian cut down for disobedience.

sentences.zh: 都尉復還戰，立引兵救之。

translations.literal: The commandant again returned to fight; Chen Li led troops to rescue him.
translations.idiomatic: Wannian wheeled back into the fight; Chen Li threw in reinforcements.

sentences.zh: 時天大旱，立攻絕其水道。

translations.literal: At the time heaven sent great drought; Li attacked and cut off their watercourse.
translations.idiomatic: A crushing drought settled in; Chen Li seized the rebels’ water and strangled them.

sentences.zh: 蠻夷共斬翁指，持首出降。

translations.literal: The barbarian Yi together beheaded Wuzhi, carried his head forth, and surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: The Yi turned on Wuzhi, presented his head, and capitulated.

sentences.zh: 立已平定西夷，征詣京師。

translations.literal: Chen Li having already pacified the western Yi, was summoned to proceed to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: With the western Yi quiet, the court recalled Chen Li to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 會巴郡有盜賊，復以立爲巴郡太守，秩中二千石居，賜爵左庶長。

translations.literal: It happened that Ba commandery had bandits; Chen Li was again appointed grand administrator of Ba commandery, with salary rank at full two thousand shi, and was granted the noble rank of Left Senior Leader.
translations.idiomatic: Banditry in Ba bought him a second tour as governor—full two-thousand-shi salary and the noble rank of Left Senior Leader.

sentences.zh: 徙爲天水太守，勸民農桑爲天下最，賜金四十斤。

translations.literal: He was transferred to be grand administrator of Tianshui; he urged the people in farming and sericulture to be foremost in All-under-Heaven, and was granted forty jin of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Shifted to Tianshui, he made its farms and mulberry groves a model for the empire and earned forty jin of gold.

sentences.zh: 入爲左曹衛將軍、護軍都尉，卒官。

translations.literal: He entered as Left Bureau Guards General and Colonel Protecting the Army, and died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He finished as Left Bureau Guards general and colonel of the army guard—dying in harness.

sentences.zh: 王莽篡位，改漢制，貶鉤町王以爲侯。

translations.literal: Wang Mang usurped the throne, altered Han institutions, and degraded the King of Gouding to be a marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang seized the throne, rewrote Han law, and stripped Gouding’s king down to marquis rank.

sentences.zh: 王邯怨恨，牂柯大尹周欽詐殺邯。

translations.literal: King Han harbored resentment; Grand Intendant of Zangke Zhou Qin treacherously killed Han.
translations.idiomatic: King Han nursed a grudge; Zangke’s Grand Intendant Zhou Qin lured him to his death.

sentences.zh: 邯弟承攻殺欽，州郡擊之，不能服。

translations.literal: Han’s younger brother Cheng attacked and killed Qin; provincial commanderies struck them but could not subdue them.
translations.idiomatic: Han’s brother Cheng avenged him on Qin; provincial armies could not bring the south to heel.

sentences.zh: 三邊蠻夷愁擾盡反，復殺益州大尹程隆。

translations.literal: The barbarian Yi on three frontiers, aggrieved and stirred, all rebelled, and again killed Grand Intendant of Yizhou Cheng Long.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier Yi rose everywhere in fury and slew Yizhou’s Grand Intendant Cheng Long.

sentences.zh: 莽遣平蠻將軍馮茂發巴、蜀、犍爲吏士，賦斂取足於民，以擊益州。

translations.literal: Mang dispatched Pacification-of-the-Barbarians General Feng Mao to mobilize clerks and soldiers from Ba, Shu, and Qianwei, levying taxes until sufficient was taken from the people, to strike Yizhou.
translations.idiomatic: Mang sent Feng Mao south with Ba–Shu–Qianwei troops, wringing the countryside until his purse was full, then marched on Yizhou.

sentences.zh: 出入三年，疾疫死者什七，巴、蜀騷動。

translations.literal: Going in and out three years, those dead of sickness and plague were seven-tenths; Ba and Shu were in turmoil.
translations.idiomatic: Three years of campaigning killed seven men in ten by plague; Ba and Shu buckled under the strain.

sentences.zh: 莽征茂還，誅之。

translations.literal: Mang summoned Mao back and executed him.
translations.idiomatic: Mang recalled Feng Mao and took his head.

sentences.zh: 更遣甯始將軍廉丹與庸部牧史熊大發天水、隴西騎士，廣漢、巴、蜀、犍爲吏民十萬人，轉輸者合二十萬人，擊之。

translations.literal: He further dispatched General-in-Chief at Rest Lian Dan and Pasturer of the Yong department Shi Xiong to mobilize horsemen from Tianshui and Longxi, one hundred thousand clerks and commoners from Guanghan, Ba, Shu, and Qianwei, transport personnel totaling two hundred thousand, and strike them.
translations.idiomatic: Next came Lian Dan and Yong department’s Shi Xiong with Longxi–Tianshui cavalry, a hundred thousand levies from Guanghan through Qianwei, and twice that many porters.

sentences.zh: 始至，頗斬首數千，其後軍糧前後不相及，士卒饑疫，三歲余死者數萬。

translations.literal: At first arrival they beheaded several thousand in fair measure; afterward army grain fore and aft did not link up, soldiers starved and sickened in plague, and after more than three years the dead numbered in the tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: Early raids scored thousands of heads; then supply trains failed, hunger and pestilence swept the camps, and within three years tens of thousands were dead.

sentences.zh: 而粵巂蠻夷任貴亦殺太守枚根，自立爲邛谷王。

translations.literal: Moreover the barbarian Yi Ren Gui of Yuexi also killed Grand Administrator Mei Gen and established himself as King of Qionggu.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Yuexi’s Yi leader Ren Gui slew Governor Mei Gen and styled himself king of the Qiong valley.

sentences.zh: 會莽敗漢興，誅貴，復舊號雲。

translations.literal: It happened that Mang was defeated and Han rose; Ren Gui was executed, and old titles were restored, as the saying goes.
translations.idiomatic: Mang fell, Han returned, Ren Gui was executed, and the former titles were restored.

sentences.zh: 南粵王趙佗，真定人也。

translations.literal: The King of Southern Yue Zhao Tuo was a man of Zhending.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Tuo, king of Southern Yue, hailed from Zhending.

sentences.zh: 秦並天下，略定揚粵，置桂林、南海、象郡，以適徙民與粵雜處。

translations.literal: When Qin annexed All-under-Heaven, it roughly settled Yang–Yue, established Guilin, Nanhai, and Xiang commanderies, and by banishment-transfers settled commoners to dwell intermixed with Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Unified Qin carved out Guilin, Nanhai, and Xiang from the Yang–Yue south and parked convict colonists among the Yue.

sentences.zh: 十三歲，至二世時，南海尉任囂病且死，召龍川令趙佗語曰：「聞陳勝等作亂，豪桀叛秦相立，南海辟遠，恐盜兵侵此。

translations.literal: Thirteen years on, in the time of the Second Emperor, Nanhai Commandant Ren Xiao was ill and about to die; he summoned Longchuan Magistrate Zhao Tuo and said to him: ‘I have heard Chen Sheng and others raised rebellion; heroes betray Qin and set one another up as lords; Nanhai is remote— I fear bandit troops will invade here.’
translations.idiomatic: Thirteen years later, dying Nanhai commandant Ren Xiao called in Longchuan’s Zhao Tuo: rebels like Chen Sheng were toppling Qin; this distant coast might be next.’

sentences.zh: 吾欲興兵絕新道，自備侍諸侯變，會疾甚。

translations.literal: I wish to raise troops to sever the new roads, prepare myself and await the feudal lords’ changes—only my illness is severe.’
translations.idiomatic: I meant to seal the new highways and wait out the warlords—but this sickness has me.’

sentences.zh: 且番禺負山險阻，南北東西數千里，頗有中國人相輔，此亦一州之主，可爲國。

translations.literal: Moreover Panyu backs onto mountains and perilous barriers; north, south, east, and west span several thousand li, and there are quite a few Central States people to assist—this too is master of one province; it can become a state.’
translations.idiomatic: Panyu sits behind ranges for thousands of li in every direction, with Han settlers to lean on—enough to be a regional power.’

sentences.zh: 郡中長吏亡足與謀者，故召公告之。

translations.literal: Among senior clerks in the commandery there is none worth plotting with; therefore I summon you, lord, and tell you.’
translations.idiomatic: No one else in this command is fit to plan with—that is why I called you.’

sentences.zh: 」即被佗書，行南海尉事。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon he issued Tuo a written commission to exercise Nanhai Commandant’s affairs.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He handed Zhao Tuo acting command of Nanhai.

sentences.zh: 囂死，佗即移檄告橫浦、陽山、湟溪關曰：「盜兵且至，急絕道聚兵自守。

translations.literal: When Xiao died, Tuo immediately shifted proclamations informing Hengpu, Yangshan, and Huangxi Passes: ‘Bandit troops are about to arrive; urgently sever the roads, gather troops, and defend yourselves.’
translations.idiomatic: Xiao died; Zhao Tuo wired the mountain passes—bandits coming; bar the roads and rally the garrisons.’

sentences.zh: 」因稍以法誅秦所置吏，以其黨爲守假。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon gradually by law he executed officials Qin had placed, and made his faction acting guardians.
translations.idiomatic: ’ Then he lawfully purged Qin’s appointees and filled posts with his own men.

sentences.zh: 秦已滅，佗即擊並桂林、象郡，自立爲南粵武王。

translations.literal: When Qin was already destroyed, Tuo thereupon attacked and annexed Guilin and Xiang commanderies, and established himself as Martial King of Southern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Qin gone, Zhao Tuo swallowed Guilin and Xiang and proclaimed himself Southern Yue’s Martial King.

sentences.zh: 高帝已定天下，爲中國勞苦，故釋佗不誅。

translations.literal: High Emperor had already settled All-under-Heaven; for the Central States’ weariness and toil, therefore he released Tuo without executing him.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu had pacified the realm but shrank from another costly campaign—he spared Zhao Tuo.

sentences.zh: 十一年，遣陸賈立佗爲南粵王，與部符通使，使和輯百粵，毋爲南邊害，與長沙接境。

translations.literal: In the eleventh year he dispatched Lu Jia to establish Tuo as King of Southern Yue, divided tally-credentials and opened envoys, ordering him to harmonize and gather the hundred Yue, not become harm on the southern frontier, bordering Changsha.
translations.idiomatic: In year eleven Lu Jia invested Zhao Tuo as king of Southern Yue, restored tallied missions, and charged him to pacify the Yue peoples without troubling Han’s south—his realm adjoined Changsha.

sentences.zh: 高後時，有司請禁粵關市鐵器。

translations.literal: In Empress Gao’s time, officials requested prohibiting Yue from frontier-market trade in iron implements.
translations.idiomatic: Under Empress Lü the bureaucracy moved to ban iron sales to Yue at the passes.

sentences.zh: 佗曰：「高皇帝立我，通使物，今高後聽讒臣，別異蠻夷，隔絕器物，此必長沙王計，欲倚中國，擊滅南海並王之，自爲功也。

translations.literal: Tuo said: ‘High Emperor established me and exchanged envoys and goods; now Empress Gao listens to slanderous ministers, treats barbarian Yi as different, severs and isolates tools and goods—this must be the King of Changsha’s plot; he wishes to lean on the Central States, strike and destroy Nanhai, annex and rule it together, and make achievement for himself.’
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Tuo fumed: Gaozu had recognized him and kept trade flowing; Lü’s court listened to malice, singled out the barbarians, and embargoed metal—classic Changsha intrigue to borrow Han blades and swallow his kingdom.’

sentences.zh: 」於是佗乃自尊號爲南武帝，發兵攻長沙邊，敗數縣焉。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon Tuo then honored himself with the title Martial Emperor of the South, raised troops and attacked Changsha’s border, and defeated several counties.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He crowned himself Southern Martial Emperor and struck Changsha’s frontier counties.

sentences.zh: 高後遣將軍隆慮侯灶擊之，會暑濕，士卒大疫，兵不能逾領。

translations.literal: Empress Gao dispatched General Marquis of Longlü Zao to strike them; it happened to be hot and damp, the soldiers greatly sickened in plague, and troops could not cross the ridge.
translations.idiomatic: Lü sent Marquis Zao of Longlü; heat and malaria hollowed the column—it never crossed the divide.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，高後崩，即罷兵。

translations.literal: After more than a year Empress Gao died, and at once they ceased arms.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Empress Lü died and Han called off the expedition.

sentences.zh: 佗因此以兵威財物賂遺閩粵、西甌駱，伇屬焉。

translations.literal: Tuo by this with military might and goods bribed and gifted Minyue and Western Ou Luo, bringing them into dependent service.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Tuo spent treasure and swagger to pull Minyue and the western Ou-Luo into his orbit.

sentences.zh: 東西萬餘里。

translations.literal: East and west more than ten thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: His sway stretched ten thousand li from east to west.

sentences.zh: 乃乘黃屋左纛，稱制，與中國侔。

translations.literal: He then rode the yellow canopy and left-banner pennons, issued edicts in his own right, and rivaled the Central States.
translations.idiomatic: He adopted the imperial equipage, issued orders as sovereign, and matched the Middle Kingdom in pomp.

sentences.zh: 文帝元年，初鎮撫天下，使告諸侯四夷從代來即位意，諭盛德焉。

translations.literal: In the first year of Emperor Wen, at first he pacified and soothed All-under-Heaven, sent to inform the feudal lords and four Yi of the intent behind his accession from Dai, instructing them in abundant virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen’s first year opened with outreach: he explained his rise from Dai to every prince and frontier power and showcased his virtue.

sentences.zh: 乃爲佗親塚在真定置守邑，歲時奉祀。

translations.literal: He thereupon for Tuo’s parents’ tombs in Zhending set up a guard settlement, with seasonal offerings.
translations.idiomatic: He posted a guard village at Zhao Tuo’s ancestral graves in Zhending and funded regular sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 召其從昆弟，尊官厚賜寵之。

translations.literal: He summoned his collateral cousins, honoring them with high office, thick gifts, and favor.
translations.idiomatic: He summoned Zhao’s cousins northward, gave them rank, silk, and favor.

sentences.zh: 召丞相平舉可使粵者，平言陸賈先帝時使粵。

translations.literal: He summoned Chancellor Ping to recommend one who could be sent to Yue; Ping said Lu Jia in the former emperor’s time had been sent to Yue.
translations.idiomatic: He asked Chancellor Chen Ping for an envoy; Ping named Lu Jia, who had served under Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 上召賈爲太中大夫，謁者一人爲副使，賜佗書曰：「皇帝謹問南粵王，甚苦心勞意。

translations.literal: The emperor summoned Jia as Grandee of the Palace, one Herald as deputy envoy, and granted Tuo a letter saying: ‘The Emperor respectfully inquires of the King of Southern Yue; you have been very bitter in mind and toilsome in effort.’
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia became Grandee of the Palace with a herald as deputy; the emperor’s letter opened: ‘The Son of Heaven greets the king of Southern Yue and knows how heavy your cares have been.’

sentences.zh: 朕，高皇帝側室之子，棄外奉北籓於代，道里遼遠，壅蔽樸愚，未嘗致書。

translations.literal: I am a son by a side chamber of High Emperor, cast aside to hold the northern fief of Dai; the road is far, I was blocked off plain and foolish, and never sent letter.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘I am a minor son of Gaozu, long relegated to Dai’s northern march—remote, rustic, and silent until now.’

sentences.zh: 高皇帝棄群臣，孝惠皇帝即世，高後自臨事，不幸有疾，日進不衰，以故悖暴乎治。

translations.literal: High Emperor abandoned the host of ministers; Emperor Xiaohui succeeded to the throne; Empress Gao herself attended to affairs; unluckily she had illness, daily advancing without decline, and therefore governance turned contrary and violent.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Gaozu died; Emperor Hui followed; Empress Lü ruled in person while sickness twisted her judgment until government ran harsh and skewed.’

sentences.zh: 諸呂爲變故亂法，不能獨制，乃取它姓子爲孝惠皇帝嗣。

translations.literal: The various Lüs made turmoil and disrupted law; unable to control alone, she took a child of another surname as heir to Emperor Xiaohui.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘The Lü clan bent the statutes; unable to rule alone, she installed an outsider as Hui’s heir.’

sentences.zh: 賴宗廟之靈，功臣之力，誅之已畢。

translations.literal: Relying on the ancestral temples’ spirits and the merit ministers’ strength, they have been executed and it is finished.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Temple spirits and founding ministers prevailed—the plotters are dead.’

sentences.zh: 朕以王侯吏不釋之故，不得不立，今即位。

translations.literal: I because kings, marquises, and clerks would not release the matter, had no choice but to establish myself; now I have assumed the throne.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Princes and officials would brook no delay—I took the throne because I had to.’

sentences.zh: 乃者聞王遺將軍隆慮侯書，求親昆弟，請罷長沙兩將軍。

translations.literal: Recently I heard the king sent a letter to General Marquis of Longlü, seeking close cousins and requesting dismissal of Changsha’s two generals.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘I hear you wrote Marquis Zao of Longlü for kin in the north and asked Han to pull Changsha’s two generals.’

sentences.zh: 朕以王書罷將軍博陽侯，親昆弟在真定者，已遣人存問，修治先人塚。

translations.literal: I by the king’s letter dismissed General Marquis of Boyang; those close cousins in Zhending I have already dispatched people to inquire after and repair the tombs of forebears.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘On your word I cashiered Marquis Boyang; agents already tend your kin and ancestral graves in Zhending.’

sentences.zh: 前日聞王發兵于邊，爲寇災不止。

translations.literal: The other day I heard the king raised troops on the border, as raiding calamity without cease.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Yet lately your troops raid the frontier without end.’

sentences.zh: 當其時，長沙苦之，南郡尤甚，雖王之國，庸獨利乎！

translations.literal: At that time Changsha suffered from it, Nan commandery especially severely—even the king’s state, how could it alone profit!’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Changsha bleeds—Nan commandery worst of all—even your own realm gains nothing by this!’

sentences.zh: 必多殺士卒，傷良將吏，寡人之妻，孤人之子，獨人父母，得一亡十，朕不忍爲也。

translations.literal: Surely many soldiers will be killed, fine generals and clerks wounded, wives widowed, sons orphaned, parents bereaved of children—gaining one and losing ten; I cannot bear to do it.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘War means stacks of widows, orphans, and childless elders—one village taken for ten ruined counties; I will not pay that coin gladly.’

sentences.zh: 朕欲定地犬牙相入者，以問吏，吏曰『高皇帝所以介長沙土也』，朕不得擅變焉。

translations.literal: I wished to fix lands that interlock like dogs’ teeth; I asked clerks, and clerks said ‘This is why High Emperor bordered Changsha’s territory’; I may not arbitrarily alter it.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘I asked to straighten the zigzag border; clerks answered that Gaozu drew those teeth—I cannot redraw them alone.’

sentences.zh: 吏曰：『得王之地不足以爲大，得王之財不足以爲富，服領以南，王自治之。

translations.literal: Clerks said: ‘Taking the king’s lands is insufficient to count as great, taking the king’s wealth insufficient to count as rich; south of Fuling, let the king rule himself.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘They add: your lands would not enlarge Han nor your treasure enrich us—south of the ridge you may govern yourself.’

sentences.zh: 』雖然，王之號爲帝。

translations.literal: ’ Although it is so, the king’s title is emperor.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Yet you still style yourself emperor.’

sentences.zh: 兩帝並立，亡一乘之使以通其道，是爭也；

translations.literal: Two emperors standing together, without one carriage envoy to open their way—this is contention;’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Two Son of Heaven without envoys between them is rivalry—’

sentences.zh: 爭而不讓，仁者不爲也。

translations.literal: To contend without yielding—the benevolent do not do it.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘—and rivalry without yielding ill suits humane rule.’

sentences.zh: 願與王分棄前患，終今以來，通使如故。

translations.literal: I wish with the king together to cast aside prior grievances; from the end of now onward, envoys as of old.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Let us bury old scores from this day and exchange envoys as before.’

sentences.zh: 故使賈馳諭告王朕意，王亦受之，毋爲寇災矣。

translations.literal: Therefore I send Jia galloping to instruct and announce to the king my intent; the king should also accept it—do not be raiding calamity.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Lu Jia rides south with this olive branch—accept it and spare us border raids.’

sentences.zh: 上褚五十衣，中褚三十衣，下褚二十衣，遺王。

translations.literal: Fifty upper-grade garments, thirty middle-grade garments, twenty lower-grade garments—presented to the king.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘I send fifty upper suits, thirty middle, twenty lower.’

sentences.zh: 願王聽樂娛憂，存問鄰國。」

translations.literal: May the king listen to music to ease worry, and inquire after neighboring states.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘May music lighten your cares and courtesy bind your neighbors.’

sentences.zh: 陸賈至，南粵王恐，乃頓首謝，願奉明詔，長爲籓臣，奉貢職。

translations.literal: When Lu Jia arrived, the King of Southern Yue feared; he then knocked his head in apology, wished to uphold the enlightened edict, long be a frontier vassal, and observe tribute duties.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia’s arrival rattled Zhao Tuo; he kowtowed, pledged to obey the decree, and resume life as a tributary king.

sentences.zh: 於是下令國中曰：「吾聞兩雄不俱立，兩賢不並世。

translations.literal: Thereupon he issued order within the state, saying: ‘I have heard two heroes do not stand together, two worthies do not share one age.’
translations.idiomatic: He proclaimed through Yue: ‘Two stallions cannot share one stable—two sages cannot rule one age.’

sentences.zh: 漢皇帝賢天子。

translations.literal: The Han emperor is a worthy Son of Heaven.’
translations.idiomatic: The Han Son of Heaven is the true sage-king.’

sentences.zh: 自今以來，去帝制黃屋左纛。

translations.literal: From now onward, cast off imperial institutions—the yellow canopy and left-banner pennons.’
translations.idiomatic: Henceforth I shed imperial title, yellow canopy, and kingly pennant.’

sentences.zh: 」因爲書稱：「蠻夷大長老夫臣佗昧死再拜上書皇帝陛下：老夫故粵吏也，高皇帝幸賜臣佗璽，以爲南粵王，使爲外臣，時內貢職。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon he composed a document stating: ‘Great elder of the barbarian Yi, your aged servant Tuo, risking death twice bows and submits memorial to His Majesty the Emperor: your aged servant was formerly a Yue clerk; High Emperor deigned to grant your servant Tuo a seal, making him King of Southern Yue, ordering him to be outer vassal, from time to time rendering inner tribute duties.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ His memorial ran: ‘Barbarian elder Zhao Tuo kowtows to the throne: once a mere Yue official, I received Gaozu’s seal as king of Southern Yue and outer vassal with regular tribute inward.’

sentences.zh: 孝惠皇帝即位，義不忍絕，所以賜老夫者厚甚。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaohui assumed the throne, by righteousness he could not bear to sever ties; therefore what he granted your aged servant was very generous.’
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Hui succeeded and out of kindness kept the gifts flowing—lavishly.’

sentences.zh: 高後自臨用事，近細士，信讒臣，別異蠻夷，出令曰：『毋予蠻夷外粵金鐵田器；

translations.literal: Empress Gao herself attended to affairs, drew near petty men, trusted slanderous ministers, treated barbarian Yi as distinct, and issued order saying: ‘Do not give barbarian Yi outer Yue metal farm tools;’
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü ruled through petty favorites who issued edicts: no iron farm tools to outer Yue barbarians;’

sentences.zh: 馬、牛、羊即予，予牡，毋與牝。

translations.literal: Horses, cattle, sheep—if given, give males; do not give females.’
translations.idiomatic: If livestock went north, send only males—never breeding stock.’

sentences.zh: 』老夫處辟，馬、羊、羊齒已長，自以祭祀不修，有死罪，使內史籓、中尉高、御史平凡三輩上書謝過，皆不反。

translations.literal: ’ Your aged servant dwells in remote lands; horses and sheep—their teeth already grown long—your servant deemed sacrifices ill tended and held himself guilty of capital crime; he dispatched Interior Clerk Fan, Commandant Gao, and Clerk Ping Fan in three parties to submit memorials apologizing for faults—none received reply.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ Stranded in the south with aging herds, I feared my neglect of ancestral rites was treason—I sent Fan, Gao, and Ping thrice with apologies; silence answered each.’

sentences.zh: 又風聞老夫父母墳墓已壞削，兄弟宗族已誅論。

translations.literal: Moreover by rumor your aged servant heard parents’ graves already destroyed and leveled, brothers and clan kin already condemned and executed.’
translations.idiomatic: Rumor said Han had smashed my parents’ tombs and executed my kin.’

sentences.zh: 吏相與議曰：『今內不得振於漢。

translations.literal: Clerks consulted together saying: ‘Now inwardly one cannot gain standing from Han.’
translations.idiomatic: My advisers muttered: ‘Han gives us no dignity within—’

sentences.zh: 外亡以自高異。

translations.literal: Outwardly there is no means to elevate oneself as distinct.’
translations.idiomatic: —and abroad no lever to raise our prestige.’

sentences.zh: 』故更號爲帝，自帝其國，非敢有害於天下也。

translations.literal: ’ Therefore I changed title to emperor and declared myself emperor of my state—not daring to intend harm to All-under-Heaven.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ So I borrowed an emperor’s title at home—not to threaten the realm.’

sentences.zh: 高皇后聞之大怒，削去南粵之籍，使使不通。

translations.literal: Empress Gao heard it and was greatly angry; she struck Southern Yue from the registers and cut off envoys from passage.’
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü struck Yue from the rolls and sealed the missions.’

sentences.zh: 老夫竊疑長沙王讒臣，故敢發兵以伐其邊。

translations.literal: Your aged servant privately suspected the King of Changsha’s slanderous ministers; therefore he dared raise troops to strike his border.’
translations.idiomatic: I blamed Changsha’s faction and hit their frontier.’

sentences.zh: 且南方卑濕，蠻夷中西有西甌，其衆半羸，南面稱王；

translations.literal: Moreover the south is low and damp; among barbarian Yi to the west there is Western Ou, their hosts half emaciated, yet facing south they style themselves kings;’
translations.idiomatic: The muggy south breeds rivals: Western Ou, half starved, still crowns itself king in the west;’

sentences.zh: 東有閩粵，其衆數千人，亦稱王；

translations.literal: eastward there is Minyue, their hosts several thousand men, also styling themselves kings;’
translations.idiomatic: eastward Minyue’s thousands call themselves kings;’

sentences.zh: 西北有長沙，其半蠻夷，亦稱王。

translations.literal: northwest there is Changsha, half of it barbarian Yi, also styling themselves kings.’
translations.idiomatic: northwest Changsha—half Yi—does the same.’

sentences.zh: 老夫故敢妄竊帝號，聊以自娛。

translations.literal: Therefore your aged servant dared rashly to steal an emperor’s title, merely to amuse himself.’
translations.idiomatic: Small wonder I toyed with an empty emperor’s style.’

sentences.zh: 老夫身定百邑之地，東西南北數千萬里，帶甲百萬有餘，然北面而臣事漢，何也？

translations.literal: Your aged servant personally settled a hundred settlements’ lands, east, west, south, north several tens of millions of li, armored troops more than a million—yet faces north and serves Han as subject—why?’
translations.idiomatic: I carved out a million spears and myriad li—yet I bow north to Han: why?’

sentences.zh: 不敢背先人之故。

translations.literal: Your aged servant dares not betray the precedent of forebears.’
translations.idiomatic: Because I dare not dishonor what my predecessors began.’

sentences.zh: 老夫處粵四十九年，於今抱孫焉。

translations.literal: Your aged servant has dwelt in Yue forty-nine years; by now he holds grandsons.’
translations.idiomatic: Forty-nine years in Yue—I am a grandfather now.’

sentences.zh: 然夙興夜寐，寢不安席，食不甘味，目不視靡曼之色，耳不聽鐘鼓之音者，以不得事漢也。

translations.literal: Yet rising early and retiring late, sleeping without ease on the mat, eating without savor in taste, eyes not viewing sumptuous colors, ears not listening to bell-drum music—it is because one could not serve Han.’
translations.idiomatic: Yet I rose before dawn and slept uneasy—no taste for food, no taste for silk or music—all because Han shut its door on me.’

sentences.zh: 今陛下幸哀憐，復故號，通使漢如故，老夫死骨不腐，改號不敢爲帝矣！

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty deigns pity; restores former titles; opens envoys with Han as of old—your aged servant’s dead bones will not rot; changing titles he dares not be emperor!’
translations.idiomatic: Now Your Majesty restores my old station and the old traffic—my bones may rest easy; I shall never again style myself emperor!’

sentences.zh: 謹北面因使者獻白璧一雙，翠鳥千，犀角十，紫貝五百，桂蠹一器，生翠四十雙，孔雀二雙。

translations.literal: Respectfully facing north by the envoy’s route he presents paired white jade disks, one thousand kingfishers, ten rhinoceros horns, five hundred purple cowries, one vessel of cassia-borer specimens, forty pairs of raw kingfisher feathers, two pairs of peacocks.’
translations.idiomatic: Through your envoy I send paired white jades, a thousand kingfisher skins, ten rhino horns, five hundred cowries, a jar of cinnamon larvae, forty pairs of kingfisher plumes, and two pairs of peacocks.’

sentences.zh: 昧死再拜，以聞皇帝陛下。」

translations.literal: Risking death twice bowing, thereby to inform His Majesty the Emperor.’
translations.idiomatic: Risking execution, I bow twice and lay this before the throne.’

sentences.zh: 陸賈還報，文帝大說。

translations.literal: Lu Jia returned with report; Emperor Wen was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Jia’s report delighted Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 遂至孝景時，稱臣遣使入朝請。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Xiaojing’s time, declaring himself subject he dispatched envoys to enter court audience with requests.
translations.idiomatic: Through Emperor Jing’s reign he styled himself vassal and sent envoys to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 然其居國，竊如故號；

translations.literal: Yet in dwelling in his state, he privately kept his former title;
translations.idiomatic: At home he quietly kept his old imperial style;

sentences.zh: 其使天子，稱王朝命如諸侯。

translations.literal: when his envoys went to the Son of Heaven, they declared kingly court commands like the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: his missions to the capital spoke only the language of a feudal king.

sentences.zh: 至武帝建元四年，佗孫胡爲南粵王。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Wu’s fourth Jianyuan year, Tuo’s grandson Hu became King of Southern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: By Wu-di’s Jianyuan 4, Zhao Tuo’s grandson Hu ruled Southern Yue.

sentences.zh: 立三年，閩粵王郢興兵南擊邊邑。

translations.literal: Three years after his establishment, the King of Minyue Ying raised troops and struck south at border settlements.
translations.idiomatic: Three years into his reign, Minyue’s king Ying swept south against Yue’s march towns.

sentences.zh: 粵使人上書曰：「兩粵俱爲籓臣，毋擅興兵相攻擊。

translations.literal: Yue sent men to submit memorial saying: ‘The two Yues are both frontier vassals; do not dare raise troops on your own authority to attack one another.’
translations.idiomatic: Yue petitioned: ‘Both realms are Han vassals—no private wars between us.’

sentences.zh: 今東粵擅興兵侵臣，臣不敢興兵，唯天子詔之。

translations.literal: Now Eastern Yue on its own authority raises troops and invades your servant; your servant does not dare raise troops—only the Son of Heaven may issue edict on it.’
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Yue struck first; I refuse to answer in kind—only the Son of Heaven may judge.’

sentences.zh: 」於是天子多南粵義，守職約，爲興師，遣兩將軍往討閩粵。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon the Son of Heaven praised Southern Yue’s righteousness, guarded duty and covenant, and for them raised armies, dispatching two generals to proceed and punish Minyue.
translations.idiomatic: ’ The court praised Yue’s restraint, honored the treaty, and sent two generals against Minyue.

sentences.zh: 兵未逾領，閩粵王弟餘善殺郢以降，於是罷兵。

translations.literal: Before troops crossed the ridge, Minyue King’s younger brother Yu Shan killed Ying and surrendered; thereupon arms were halted.
translations.idiomatic: Han never crossed the divide: Minyue prince Yu Shan murdered King Ying and yielded—so Han stood down.

sentences.zh: 相呂嘉年長矣，相三王，宗族官貴爲長吏七十餘人，男盡尚王女，女盡嫁王子弟宗室，及蒼梧秦王有連。

translations.literal: Chancellor Lü Jia was advanced in years; he had served as chancellor to three kings; clan kin holding noble office counted more than seventy senior clerks; the men all married royal daughters, the women all married royal sons and younger brothers of the imperial house; moreover he had marital ties to the Prince of Cangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Jia, elderly premier to three Yue kings, had seventy-odd kinsmen in high posts, sons wedded to princesses and daughters to Zhao royals, plus marriage ties to Cangwu’s prince.

sentences.zh: 其居國中甚重，粵人信之，多爲耳目者，得衆心愈于王。

translations.literal: His standing within the state was very weighty; Yue people trusted him; many served as his eyes and ears; he won the host’s hearts more than the king.
translations.idiomatic: He dominated court opinion—his spy ring ran deep—and ordinary Yue favored him over their king.

sentences.zh: 王之上書，數諫止王，王不聽。

translations.literal: Regarding the king’s memorials to the throne, he repeatedly remonstrated to stop the king; the king would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the king drafted petitions northward, Lü Jia talked him down—the king rarely listened.

sentences.zh: 有畔心，數稱病不見漢使者。

translations.literal: He harbored rebel intent and repeatedly claimed illness and refused to meet Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: He nursed treason and dodged every Han envoy with feigned sickness.

sentences.zh: 使者注意嘉，勢未能誅。

translations.literal: The envoys fixed attention on Jia; circumstances did not allow executing him.
translations.idiomatic: Envoys marked Lü Jia for removal but lacked force to strike.

sentences.zh: 王、王太后亦恐嘉等先事發，欲介使者權，謀誅嘉等。

translations.literal: The king and queen dowager also feared Jia and cohort might strike first; they wished to borrow envoys’ authority and plotted to execute Jia and the rest.
translations.idiomatic: King and queen mother feared Lü Jia would move first and schemed to borrow Han envoys’ cover for a coup.

sentences.zh: 置酒請使者，大臣皆侍坐飲。

translations.literal: They set feasts and invited envoys; great ministers all attended seated and drank.
translations.idiomatic: They banqueted the Han party—all senior ministers at table.

sentences.zh: 嘉弟爲將，將卒居宮外。

translations.literal: Jia’s younger brother was a general; leading troops he lodged outside the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Jia’s brother commanded body troops camped beyond the palace walls.

sentences.zh: 酒行，太后謂嘉：「南粵內屬，國之利，而相君苦不便者，何也？

translations.literal: As wine circulated, the queen dowager said to Jia: ‘Southern Yue attaching inward is profit for the state—yet Chancellor finds it bitterly inconvenient—why?’
translations.idiomatic: Mid-feast the queen mother cornered Lü Jia: annexation helps Yue—why does the premier fight it?’

sentences.zh: 」以激怒使者。

translations.literal: ’ intending to provoke the envoys’ anger.
translations.idiomatic: —hoping to bait the Han envoys into action.

sentences.zh: 使者狐疑相杖，遂不敢發。

translations.literal: The envoys hesitated and leaned on one another; thereupon they did not dare strike.
translations.idiomatic: The envoys wavered and held each other back—no blow fell.

sentences.zh: 嘉見耳目非是，即趨出。

translations.literal: Jia saw eyes and ears were not right and immediately hurried out.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Jia read the room and bolted.

sentences.zh: 太后怒，欲鏦嘉以矛，王止太后。

translations.literal: The queen dowager grew angry and wished to spear Jia with a lance; the king stopped the queen dowager.
translations.idiomatic: The queen mother grabbed for a spear; the king restrained her.

sentences.zh: 嘉遂出，介弟兵就舍，稱病，不肯見王及使者。

translations.literal: Jia thereupon exited, screened his younger brother’s troops to quarters, claimed illness, and refused to see king or envoys.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Jia slipped out, rallied his brother’s guard, and hid behind sickness.

sentences.zh: 乃陰謀作亂。

translations.literal: Thereupon he secretly plotted rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: He began plotting open revolt.

sentences.zh: 王素亡意誅嘉，嘉知之，以故數月不發。

translations.literal: The king had never intended to execute Jia; Jia knew it; for this reason for several months he did not launch.
translations.idiomatic: The king would never kill him—Lü Jia knew it—so he waited months before moving.

sentences.zh: 太后獨欲誅嘉等，力又不能。

translations.literal: The queen dowager alone wished to execute Jia and the rest, yet lacked strength and could not.
translations.idiomatic: Only the queen mother wanted him dead—and she lacked the muscle.

sentences.zh: 天子聞之，罪使者怯亡決。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven heard of it and blamed the envoys for cowardice without resolution.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor condemned his envoys for timidity.

sentences.zh: 又以爲王、王太后已附漢，獨呂嘉爲亂，不足以興兵，欲使莊參以二千人往。

translations.literal: Moreover deeming king and queen dowager already attached to Han, only Lü Jia making turmoil—not sufficient to raise armies—he wished to send Zhuang Can with two thousand men to go.
translations.idiomatic: Figuring king and queen mother pro-Han with only Lü Jia rebellious, he thought two thousand men under Zhuang Can enough.

sentences.zh: 參曰：「以好往，數人足；

translations.literal: Can said: ‘If going in friendship, several men suffice;’
translations.idiomatic: Zhuang Can replied: ‘A peaceful visit needs only a handful;’

sentences.zh: 以武往，二千人亡足以爲也。

translations.literal: if going by force, two thousand are insufficient to accomplish it.’
translations.idiomatic: two thousand fighters will not finish the job.’

sentences.zh: 」辭不可，天子罷參兵。

translations.literal: ’ Declining as unacceptable, the Son of Heaven dismissed Can’s troops.
translations.idiomatic: He refused the commission; the emperor stood Zhuang Can down.

sentences.zh: 郟壯士故濟北相韓千秋奮曰：「以區區粵，又有王應，獨相呂嘉爲害，願得勇士三百人，必斬嘉以報。

translations.literal: A stalwart from Jia, former Chancellor of Jibei Han Qianqiu roused himself and said: ‘For mere petty Yue, moreover with the king responding—only Chancellor Lü Jia doing harm—I wish to obtain three hundred stalwarts and am sure to behead Jia to answer.’
translations.idiomatic: Han Qianqiu of Jia, ex-chancellor of Jibei, volunteered: petty Yue, a friendly king, one villainous premier—give me three hundred blades and I bring Lü Jia’s head.’

sentences.zh: 」於是天子遣千秋與王太后弟摎樂將二千人往。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon the Son of Heaven dispatched Qianqiu together with the queen dowager’s younger brother Jiu Le to lead two thousand men and go.
translations.idiomatic: The court sent Han Qianqiu and the queen mother’s brother Jiu Le with two thousand men.

sentences.zh: 入粵境，呂嘉乃遂反，下令國中曰：「王年少。

translations.literal: Entering Yue territory, Lü Jia thereupon finally rebelled, issuing order within the state saying: ‘The king is young.’
translations.idiomatic: Inside Yue’s borders Lü Jia declared revolt: ‘The king is a boy.’

sentences.zh: 太后中國人，又與使者亂，專欲內屬，盡持先王寶入獻天子以自媚，多從人，行至長安，虜賣以爲僮。

translations.literal: The queen dowager is a Central States woman; moreover she carries on disorder with envoys; she solely wishes inward attachment—she would take all former kings’ treasures to present the Son of Heaven to curry favor; many followers—arriving at Chang’an they will be seized and sold as bondservants.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘The Han-born queen mother dallies with envoys and means to drag Yue north—she would ship the royal heirlooms to flatter Chang’an and march our people there for the slave markets.’

sentences.zh: 取自脫一時利，亡顧趙氏社稷爲萬世慮之意。

translations.literal: Seeking only to extricate momentary profit—there is no thought for the Zhao house altars of soil and grain across myriad generations.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘She chases a moment’s gain with no care for Zhao’s lasting realm.’

sentences.zh: 」乃與其弟將卒攻殺太后、王，盡殺漢使者。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon with his younger brother he led troops to attack and kill queen dowager and king, and killed all Han envoys.
translations.idiomatic: He and his brother stormed the palace, slew king and queen mother, and massacred the Han mission.

sentences.zh: 遣人告蒼梧秦王及其諸郡縣，立明王長男粵妻子術陽侯建德爲王。

translations.literal: He dispatched men to inform Prince of Cangwu and the various commanderies and counties, and established Ming King’s eldest son by his Yue wife, Marquis of Shuyang Jiande, as king.
translations.idiomatic: Messengers rallied Cangwu and the counties; he enthroned Zhao Jiande, Ming-king’s eldest son by his Yue consort and Marquis of Shuyang.

sentences.zh: 而韓千秋兵之入也，破數小邑。

translations.literal: When Han Qianqiu’s troops entered, they broke several small settlements.
translations.idiomatic: Han Qianqiu’s column overran a few towns.

sentences.zh: 其後粵直開道給食，未至番禺四十里，粵以兵擊千秋等，滅之。

translations.literal: Afterward Yue opened roads straight through and supplied food; not yet forty li from reaching Panyu, Yue with troops struck Qianqiu and party and destroyed them.
translations.idiomatic: Yue feigned safe passage and supplies—then ambushed Han Qianqiu forty li short of Panyu and annihilated him.

sentences.zh: 使人函封漢使節置塞上，好爲謾辭謝罪，發兵守要害處。

translations.literal: He sent men to box-seal Han envoys’ credentials and set them on the barrier, craft fine deceitful phrases apologizing for guilt, and raised troops to guard chokepoints.
translations.idiomatic: He returned Han tally-staves in a cedar box on the border, spun polite apologies, and fortified the passes.

sentences.zh: 於是天子曰：「韓千秋雖亡成功，亦軍鋒之冠。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven said: ‘Though Han Qianqiu had no success, he is yet foremost of the army’s spear-tip.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ruled: ‘Han Qianqiu failed—but his was the vanguard’s valor.’

sentences.zh: 封其子延年爲成安侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed his son Yannian as Marquis of Cheng’an.
translations.idiomatic: He made his son Yannian Marquis of Cheng’an.

sentences.zh: 摎樂，其姊爲王太后，首願屬漢，封其子廣德爲龍侯。

translations.literal: Jiu Le—his elder sister was queen dowager—was first to wish attachment to Han; he enfeoffed his son Guangde as Marquis of Long.
translations.idiomatic: Jiu Le’s sister was queen mother and first to seek absorption—his son Guangde became Marquis of Long.

sentences.zh: 」乃赦天下，曰：「天子微弱，諸侯力政，譏臣不討賊。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon he amnestied All-under-Heaven, saying: ‘The Son of Heaven was weak; feudal lords exerted government by force; reproaching ministers did not punish rebels.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ He proclaimed a general amnesty: ‘When the throne was weak and lords fought among themselves, ministers failed to punish traitors.’

sentences.zh: 呂嘉、建德等反，自立晏如，令粵人及江淮以南樓船十萬師往討之。」

translations.literal: Lü Jia, Jiande, and others rebelled, established themselves unruffled and at ease; order Yue people and south of Yangzi–Huai towered-ship hosts of one hundred thousand divisions to proceed and punish them.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Lü Jia and Jiande sit smug in rebellion—mobilize a hundred thousand tower ships from Yue and south of the Yangzi–Huai to crush them.’

sentences.zh: 元鼎五年秋，衛尉路博德爲伏波將軍，出桂陽，下湟水；

translations.literal: In the autumn of the fifth Yuanding year, Commandant of the Guard Lu Bode was made Wave-Quelling General, exiting Guiyang and descending Huangshui;
translations.idiomatic: Yuanding 5, autumn: Commandant Lu Bode led the Wave-Quelling host from Guiyang down the Huangshui;

sentences.zh: 主爵都尉楊僕爲樓船將軍，出豫章，下橫浦；

translations.literal: Commandant of the Capital Peers Yang Pu was made Towered-Ship General, exiting Yuzhang and descending Hengpu;
translations.idiomatic: Capital Peers commandant Yang Pu’s tower ships left Yuzhang for Hengpu;

sentences.zh: 故歸義粵侯二人爲戈船、下瀨將軍，出零陵，或下離水，或抵蒼梧；

translations.literal: Two former Yue marquises who had submitted to righteousness were made Halberd-Ship and Down-Rapids generals, exiting Lingling—some descending Lishui, some reaching Cangwu;
translations.idiomatic: Two surrendered Yue marquises took halberd ships and rapids craft from Lingling—one column on the Li, one toward Cangwu;

sentences.zh: 使馳義侯因巴、蜀罪人，發夜郎兵，下牂柯江；

translations.literal: He ordered Marquis of Chiyi to use Ba and Shu convicts, mobilize Yelang troops, and descend the Zangke River;
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of Chiyi drove Ba–Shu felons and Yelang auxiliaries down the Zangke;

sentences.zh: 鹹會番禺。

translations.literal: all joining at Panyu.
translations.idiomatic: every wing aimed for Panyu.

sentences.zh: 六年冬，樓船將軍將精卒先陷尋陿，破石門，得粵船粟，因推而前，挫粵鋒，以粵數萬人待伏波將軍。

translations.literal: In the sixth year’s winter, Towered-Ship General led picked troops first to storm Xunyang, broke Stone Gate, obtained Yue boats and grain, thereupon pressed forward, blunted Yue’s spear-tip, with several tens of thousands of Yue awaiting Wave-Quelling General.
translations.idiomatic: Winter of year six: Yang Pu’s marines took Xunyang and Stone Gate, seized Yue grain barges, rolled forward, bloodied Yue’s van—while tens of thousands of defenders waited for Lu Bode.

sentences.zh: 伏波將軍將罪人，道遠後期，與樓船會乃有千餘人，遂俱進。

translations.literal: Wave-Quelling General led convicts; the road was distant and he was late; joining Towered-Ship he then had only somewhat more than a thousand men; thereupon both advanced.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bode’s penal column trailed far behind—barely a thousand men linked up with Yang Pu—yet both pushed on.

sentences.zh: 樓船居前，至番禺，建德、嘉皆城守。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship went ahead; reaching Panyu, Jiande and Jia both defended by walls.
