translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu reached Panyu first; Jiande and Lü Jia barred the gates.

sentences.zh: 樓船自擇便處，居東南面，伏波居西北面。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship chose a convenient position and occupied southeast face; Wave-Quelling occupied northwest face.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu seized the southeast sector; Lu Bode sealed the northwest.

sentences.zh: 會暮，樓船攻敗粵人，縱火燒城。

translations.literal: As dusk fell, Towered-Ship attacked and defeated Yue men and unleashed fire burning the city.
translations.idiomatic: At dusk Yang Pu stormed the walls and set Panyu ablaze.

sentences.zh: 粵素聞伏波，莫，不知其兵多少。

translations.literal: Yue had long heard of Wave-Quelling—dark, unknowing how many his troops were.
translations.idiomatic: Yue had feared Lu Bode’s name for years—they could not gauge his numbers in the gloom.

sentences.zh: 伏波乃爲營，遣使招降者，賜印綬，復縱令相招。

translations.literal: Wave-Quelling thereupon made camp, dispatched envoys to summon those who would surrender, granted seals and ribbons, and again released them to summon one another.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bode pitched camp, handed seals to defectors, and sent them back to recruit others.

sentences.zh: 樓船力攻燒敵，反驅而入伏波營中。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship forcefully attacked burning the foe; instead they drove them inward into Wave-Quelling’s camp.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu’s assault drove survivors straight into Lu Bode’s lines.

sentences.zh: 遲旦，城中皆降伏波。

translations.literal: At dawn, all within the city surrendered to Wave-Quelling.
translations.idiomatic: By dawn the whole city had capitulated to Lu Bode.

sentences.zh: 呂嘉、建德以夜與其屬數百人亡入海。

translations.literal: Lü Jia and Jiande by night with their followers several hundred fled into the sea.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Jia and Jiande slipped seaward at night with hundreds.

sentences.zh: 伏波又問降者，知嘉所之，遣人追。

translations.literal: Wave-Quelling again questioned those who surrendered, learned where Jia had gone, and dispatched men in pursuit.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bode wrung Lü Jia’s trail from prisoners and gave chase.

sentences.zh: 故其校司馬蘇弘得建德，爲海常侯；

translations.literal: His regiment Major Su Hong captured Jiande and became Marquis of Haichang;
translations.idiomatic: Su Hong, Lu Bode’s major, bagged Jiande and earned Marquis of Haichang;

sentences.zh: 粵郎都稽得嘉，爲臨蔡侯。

translations.literal: Yue gentleman Du Ji captured Jia and became Marquis of Lancai.
translations.idiomatic: the Yue courtier Du Ji caught Lü Jia and took Marquis of Lancai.

sentences.zh: 蒼梧王趙光與粵王同姓，聞漢兵至，降，爲隨桃侯。

translations.literal: Prince of Cangwu Zhao Guang shared the Yue king’s surname; hearing Han troops had arrived, he surrendered and became Marquis of Suitao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Guang of Cangwu, a kinsman of the royal house, submitted when Han arrived and was ennobled as Marquis of Suitao.

sentences.zh: 及粵揭陽令史定降漢，爲安道侯。

translations.literal: Moreover Yue Jieyang Clerk Shi Ding surrendered to Han and became Marquis of Andao.
translations.idiomatic: Jieyang clerk Shi Ding defected and became Marquis of Andao.

sentences.zh: 粵將畢取以軍降，爲膫侯。

translations.literal: Yue general Bi Qu surrendered with his army and became Marquis of Liao.
translations.idiomatic: General Bi Qu brought his command over and took Marquis of Liao.

sentences.zh: 粵桂林監居翁諭告甌駱四十余萬口降，爲湘城侯。

translations.literal: Yue Guilin intendant Ju Weng instructed and announced to Ou-Luo more than four hundred thousand persons to surrender; he became Marquis of Xiangcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Guilin’s Ju Weng talked four hundred thousand Ou-Luo into submission and earned Marquis of Xiangcheng.

sentences.zh: 戈船、下瀨將軍兵及馳義侯所發夜郎兵未下，南粵已平。

translations.literal: Halberd-ship and Down-Rapids generals’ troops and Marquis of Chiyi’s mobilized Yelang troops had not yet descended—Southern Yue was already pacified.
translations.idiomatic: The halberd fleets, rapids squadrons, and Yelang corps never arrived—Southern Yue had already fallen.

sentences.zh: 遂以其地爲儋耳、珠崖、南海、蒼梧、郁林、合浦、交止、九真、日南九郡。

translations.literal: Thereupon taking its territory they made Dan’er, Zhuya, Nanhai, Cangwu, Yulin, Hepu, Jiaozhi, Jiuzhen, and Rinan—nine commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Han carved the south into nine commanderies: Dan’er, Zhuya, Nanhai, Cangwu, Yulin, Hepu, Jiaozhi, Jiuzhen, and Rinan.

sentences.zh: 伏波將軍益封。

translations.literal: Wave-Quelling General received enlarged enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bode’s fief was expanded.

sentences.zh: 樓船將軍以推鋒陷堅爲將梁侯。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship General for thrusting the spear-tip and breaching fortifications became Marquis of Jiangliang.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu won Marquis of Jiangliang for cracking the hardest nuts.

sentences.zh: 自尉佗王凡五世，九十三歲而亡。

translations.literal: From Commandant Tuo’s kingship in all five generations, ninety-three years and it perished.
translations.idiomatic: Five kings after Zhao Tuo—ninety-three years—the line ended.

sentences.zh: 閩粵王無諸及粵東海王搖，其先皆粵王勾踐之後也，姓騶氏。

translations.literal: Minyue King Wuzhu and Eastern Sea King Yao of Yue—their ancestors were both posterity of Yue King Goujian, surname Zou.
translations.idiomatic: Minyue’s Wuzhu and Eastern Sea’s Yao traced their line to Goujian of Yue—the clan name Zou.

sentences.zh: 秦並天下，廢爲君長，以其地爲閩中郡。

translations.literal: When Qin annexed All-under-Heaven, they were demoted to chiefs and their territory was made Minzhong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Unified Qin stripped their kingship and drew Minzhong commandery around them.

sentences.zh: 及諸侯畔秦，無諸、搖率粵歸番陽令吳芮，所謂番君者也，從諸侯滅秦。

translations.literal: When the feudal lords rebelled against Qin, Wuzhu and Yao led Yue to submit to Poyang Magistrate Wu Rui—the so-called Lord of Fan—and followed the feudal lords to destroy Qin.
translations.idiomatic: When lords rose against Qin, Wuzhu and Yao brought Minyue to Wu Rui of Poyang—the ‘Lord of Fan’—and helped fell the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 當是時，項羽主命，不王也，以故不佐楚。

translations.literal: At that time Xiang Yu controlled appointments; he did not enfeoff them as kings; for this reason they did not assist Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu handed out titles but skipped them—so they withheld aid from Chu.

sentences.zh: 漢擊項籍，無諸、搖帥粵人佐漢。

translations.literal: When Han struck Xiang Ji, Wuzhu and Yao led Yue people to assist Han.
translations.idiomatic: When Han struck Xiang Yu, Wuzhu and Yao threw Minyue behind Liu Bang.

sentences.zh: 漢五年，復立無諸爲閩粵王，王閩中故地，都冶。

translations.literal: Han fifth year, Wuzhu was again established as King of Minyue, ruling Minzhong’s former territory, capital at Ye.
translations.idiomatic: In Han’s fifth year he restored Wuzhu as king of Minyue with capital at Ye.

sentences.zh: 孝惠三年，舉高帝時粵功，曰閩君搖功多，其民便附，乃立搖爲東海王，都東甌，世號曰東甌王。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaohui third year, citing Yue merit from Gaodi’s time, said Lord of Min Yao had much merit and his people readily attached; thereupon Yao was established as King of Eastern Sea, capital at Eastern Ou, generationally titled King of Eastern Ou.
translations.idiomatic: In Hui-di year three the court cited wartime service: Yao had earned his people’s loyalty—Han named him king of Eastern Sea at Dong’ou, henceforth ‘King of Eastern Ou.’

sentences.zh: 後數世，孝景三年，吳王濞反，欲從閩粵，閩粵未肯行，獨東甌從。

translations.literal: After several generations, Emperor Xiaojing third year, Prince of Wu Pi rebelled and wished Minyue to follow; Minyue would not consent to march—only Eastern Ou followed.
translations.idiomatic: Generations later, in Jing-di year three, Liu Pi of Wu sought Minyue’s alliance—Minyue refused; only Eastern Ou marched with him.

sentences.zh: 及吳破，東甌受漢購，殺吳王丹徒，以故得不誅。

translations.literal: When Wu was broken, Eastern Ou accepted Han’s reward-offer, killed the Prince of Wu at Dantu, and for this reason escaped execution.
translations.idiomatic: After Wu’s defeat Eastern Ou earned amnesty by killing Liu Pi at Dantu for Han’s bounty.

sentences.zh: 吳王子駒亡走閩粵，怨東甌殺其父，常勸閩粵擊東甌。

translations.literal: Wu Prince’s son Ju fled to Minyue, resented Eastern Ou for killing his father, and constantly urged Minyue to strike Eastern Ou.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Pi’s son Ju fled to Minyue and ceaselessly urged revenge on Eastern Ou for his father’s death.

sentences.zh: 建元三年，閩粵發兵圍東甌，東甌使人告急天子。

translations.literal: Jianyuan third year, Minyue raised troops and besieged Eastern Ou; Eastern Ou sent men to report emergency to the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianyuan 3 Minyue besieged Eastern Ou; Ou sent frantic pleas to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 天子問太尉田蚡，蚡對曰：「粵人相攻擊，固其常，不足以煩中國往救也。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven asked Grand Commandant Tian Fen; Fen replied saying: ‘Yue people attacking one another is indeed their constant—it is insufficient to weary the Central States into going to rescue.’
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu consulted Tian Fen; Fen shrugged: Yue civil wars are routine—not worth mobilizing China.’

sentences.zh: 」中大夫嚴助詰蚡，言當救。

translations.literal: ’ Palace Grandee Yan Zhu challenged Fen, stating they ought to rescue.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Zhu challenged him—intervention was owed.

sentences.zh: 天子遣助發會稽郡兵浮海救之，語具在《助傳》。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven dispatched Zhu to mobilize Kuaiji commandery troops by sea to rescue them—the words are fully in Zhu’s tradition.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ordered Yan Zhu to lift Kuaiji’s fleet—the full account is in his biography.

sentences.zh: 漢兵未至，閩粵引兵去。

translations.literal: Han troops had not yet arrived; Minyue withdrew troops.
translations.idiomatic: Before Han landed, Minyue lifted the siege.

sentences.zh: 東粵請舉國徙中國，乃悉與衆處江、淮之間。

translations.literal: Eastern Yue requested moving the whole state into the Central States; thereupon entirely with the host they dwelt between Yangzi and Huai.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Ou asked to relocate wholesale—Han resettled them between the Yangzi and Huai.

sentences.zh: 六年，閩粵擊南粵，南粵守天子約，不敢擅發兵，而以聞。

translations.literal: Sixth year, Minyue struck Southern Yue; Southern Yue guarded the Son of Heaven’s covenant, did not dare raise troops on its own authority, and reported it.
translations.idiomatic: In year six Minyue hit Southern Yue; Southern Yue—bound by treaty—held fire and petitioned the throne.

sentences.zh: 上遣大行王恢出豫章，大司農韓安國出會稽，皆爲將軍。

translations.literal: The emperor dispatched Grand Coachman Wang Hui out Yuzhang and Minister of Finance Han Anguo out Kuaiji—both as generals.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Wang Hui from Yuzhang and Han Anguo from Kuaiji—both commissioned generals.

sentences.zh: 兵未逾領，閩粵王郢發兵距險。

translations.literal: Before troops crossed the ridge, Minyue King Ying raised troops and barred the narrows.
translations.idiomatic: Han never crossed the divide: King Ying fortified the passes.

sentences.zh: 其弟餘善與宗族謀曰：「王以擅發兵，不請，故天子兵來誅。

translations.literal: His younger brother Yu Shan consulted clan kin saying: ‘The king because he raised troops without authorization, without requesting—therefore Son of Heaven’s troops come to punish.’
translations.idiomatic: Prince Yu Shan told the clan: ‘The king marched without imperial leave—that’s why Han comes.’

sentences.zh: 漢兵衆強，即幸勝之，後來益多，滅國乃止。

translations.literal: Han troops are numerous and strong—even if by luck we defeat them, later they come ever more until destroying the state only then stopping.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Han is huge—even a lucky win draws endless reinforcements until our country dies.’

sentences.zh: 今殺王以謝天子，天子罷兵，固國完。

translations.literal: Now kill the king to apologize to the Son of Heaven; the Son of Heaven will cease arms—the state will be secure and intact.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Kill the king as apology—Han withdraws and we survive.’

sentences.zh: 不聽乃力戰，不勝即亡入海。

translations.literal: If not listened to then fight with full strength; if not victorious then flee into the sea.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Refuse, and we fight to the last—or bolt seaward if we lose.’

sentences.zh: 」皆曰：「善。

translations.literal: ’ All said: ‘Good.’
translations.idiomatic: ’ The kin agreed.

sentences.zh: 」即鏦殺王，使使奉其頭致大行。

translations.literal: ’ At once they spear-killed the king and sent envoys bearing his head to deliver to the Grand Coachman.
translations.idiomatic: They assassinated Ying and sent his head to Wang Hui.

sentences.zh: 大行曰：「所爲來者，誅王。

translations.literal: The Grand Coachman said: ‘What we came to do was punish the king.’
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hui said: ‘We crossed the mountains to execute your king.’

sentences.zh: 王頭至，不戰而殞，利莫大焉。

translations.literal: The king’s head arrived—without battle yet eliminated—profit none greater.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘His head ends the war—no greater bargain.’

sentences.zh: 」乃以便宜案兵告大司農軍，而使使奉王頭馳報天子。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon by expedient authority he stayed arms and informed Minister of Finance’s army, while dispatching envoys bearing the king’s head galloping to report the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: He froze both columns, notified Han Anguo, and galloped the king’s head to the emperor.

sentences.zh: 詔罷兩將軍兵，曰：「郢等首惡，獨無諸孫繇君醜不與謀。

translations.literal: An edict dismissed both generals’ troops, saying: ‘Ying and cohort were chief villains—only Wuzhu’s grandson Lord Yao Chou did not join the plot.’
translations.idiomatic: An edict stood down both armies: Ying led the conspiracy—only royal grandson Yao Chou stayed clean.’

sentences.zh: 」乃使郎中將立醜爲粵繇王，奉閩粵祭祀。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon he ordered Colonel of the Gentlemen to establish Chou as Yue King Yao, tending Minyue sacrifices.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He named Yao Chou king of Minyue’s ritual line.

sentences.zh: 餘善以殺郢，威行國中，民多屬，竊自立爲王，繇王不能制。

translations.literal: Yu Shan by killing Ying had prestige running through the state; the people largely attached to him; he secretly established himself as king; King Yao could not control him.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Shan’s regicide made him king in all but name—the puppet Yao could not rein him in.

sentences.zh: 上聞之，爲餘善不足復興師，曰：「餘善首誅郢，師得不勞。

translations.literal: The emperor heard of it; deeming Yu Shan insufficient to again raise armies, he said: ‘Yu Shan was first to kill Ying—the army obtained gain without toil.’
translations.idiomatic: The court judged another campaign wasteful: ‘Yu Shan rid us of Ying without battle.’

sentences.zh: 」因立餘善爲東粵王，與繇王並處。

translations.literal: ’ Thereupon he established Yu Shan as King of Eastern Yue, dwelling alongside King Yao.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He recognized Yu Shan as king of Eastern Yue beside Yao Chou.

sentences.zh: 至元鼎五年，南粵反，餘善上書請以卒八千從樓船擊呂嘉等。

translations.literal: Down to Yuanding fifth year, Southern Yue rebelled; Yu Shan submitted memorial requesting to lead eight thousand soldiers following Towered-Ship to strike Lü Jia and others.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanding 5, when Southern Yue rose, Yu Shan offered eight thousand men under Yang Pu against Lü Jia.

sentences.zh: 兵至揭陽，以海風波爲解，不行，持兩端，陰使南粵。

translations.literal: When troops reached Jieyang, citing sea winds and waves as excuse, they did not proceed; they held two ends and secretly dispatched to Southern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: At Jieyang he blamed storms and stalled—hedging while secretly dealing with Southern Yue.

sentences.zh: 及漢破番禺，樓船將軍僕上書願請引兵擊東粵。

translations.literal: When Han broke Panyu, Towered-Ship General Pu submitted memorial wishing to request leading troops to strike Eastern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: After Panyu fell, Yang Pu asked leave to march on Eastern Yue.

sentences.zh: 上以士卒勞倦，不許。

translations.literal: The emperor on grounds that soldiers were weary and exhausted did not permit it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused—his troops were spent.

sentences.zh: 罷兵，令諸校留屯豫章梅領待命。

translations.literal: He ceased arms and ordered the various colonels to remain garrisoned at Yuzhang’s Meiling awaiting orders.
translations.idiomatic: He stood the army down but left colonels camped at Meiling in Yuzhang on alert.

sentences.zh: 明年秋，餘善聞樓船請誅之，漢兵留境，且往，乃遂發兵距漢道，號將軍騶力等爲「吞漢將軍」，入白沙、武林、梅領，殺漢三校尉。

translations.literal: The next autumn Yu Shan heard Towered-Ship had requested executing him; Han troops lingered at the border about to come; thereupon he raised troops to bar Han roads, titled generals Zou Li and others ‘Swallow-Han Generals,’ entered Baisha, Wulin, and Meiling, and killed three Han colonels.
translations.idiomatic: Next autumn Yu Shan learned Yang Pu wanted his head—Han lingered on the frontier—so he seized the passes, dubbed Zou Li ‘Swallow-Han General,’ struck Baisha, Wulin, and Meiling, and slew three colonels.

sentences.zh: 是時，漢使大司農張成、故山州侯齒將屯，不敢擊，卻就便處，皆坐畏懦誅。

translations.literal: At this time Han dispatched Minister of Agriculture Zhang Cheng and former Marquis of Shanzhou Chi leading garrison troops; they did not dare strike, withdrew to convenient positions—all sat condemned for cowardice and were executed.
translations.idiomatic: Minister Zhang Cheng and Marquis Chi held static camps and fell back—all executed for cowardice.

sentences.zh: 餘善刻「武帝」璽自立，詐其民，爲妄言。

translations.literal: Yu Shan carved a ‘Martial Emperor’ seal and established himself, deceiving his people with reckless speech.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Shan forged an emperor’s seal, crowned himself, and lied to his subjects.

sentences.zh: 上遣橫海將軍韓說出句章，浮海從東方往；

translations.literal: The emperor dispatched Across-the-Sea General Han Yue out Gouzhang, crossing the sea proceeding from the east;
translations.idiomatic: Han Yue’s Across-the-Sea fleet sailed from Gouzhang eastward;

sentences.zh: 樓船將軍僕出武林，中尉王溫舒出梅領，粵侯爲戈船、下瀨將軍出如邪、白沙，元封元年冬、鹹入東粵。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship General Pu exited Wulin, Commandant of the Capital Wang Wenshu exited Meiling, Yue marquis served as Halberd-Ship and Down-Rapids generals exiting Ruye and Baisha—in Yuanfeng first year winter all entered Eastern Yue.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu struck Wulin, Wang Wenshu Meiling, Yue marquises took halberd ships from Ruye and Baisha—all converged on Eastern Yue in Yuanfeng 1 winter.

sentences.zh: 東粵素發兵距險，使徇北將軍守武林，敗樓船軍數校尉，殺長史。

translations.literal: Eastern Yue had raised troops to bar the narrows; they ordered Northward-General to guard Wulin, defeated Towered-Ship Army’s several colonels, and killed the chief clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Yue held the heights—Northward-General at Wulin broke Yang Pu’s wing and slew his chief clerk.

sentences.zh: 樓船軍卒錢唐榬終古斬徇北將軍，爲語皃侯。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship Army soldier Qiantang Mou Zhonggu beheaded Northward-General and became Marquis of Yu’er.
translations.idiomatic: Private Mou Zhonggu of Qiantang took Northward-General’s head and earned Marquis of Yu’er.

sentences.zh: 自兵未往。

translations.literal: From before troops had gone.
translations.idiomatic: This preceded the arrival of the full expedition.

sentences.zh: 故粵衍侯吳陽前在漢，漢使歸諭餘善，不聽。

translations.literal: Former Yue Marquis of Yan Wu Yang had previously been in Han; Han envoy returned instructing Yu Shan; he did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Yang of Yan marquis rank—once a hostage in Han—was sent back to sway Yu Shan; Yu Shan ignored him.

sentences.zh: 及橫海軍至，陽以其邑七百人反，攻粵軍於漢陽。

translations.literal: When Across-the-Sea Army arrived, Yang with his settlement seven hundred men rebelled, attacking Yue troops at Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Yue landed, Wu Yang rose with seven hundred locals and hit Yue forces at Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 及故粵建成侯敖與繇王居股謀，俱殺餘善，以其衆降橫海軍。

translations.literal: Moreover former Yue Marquis of Jiancheng Ao consulted King Yao Jugu; together they killed Yu Shan and with their host surrendered to Across-the-Sea Army.
translations.idiomatic: Jiancheng marquis Ao and puppet king Yao Jugu murdered Yu Shan and brought their troops to Han Yue.

sentences.zh: 封居股爲東成侯，萬戶；

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Jugu as Marquis of Dongcheng, ten thousand households;
translations.idiomatic: Jugu became Marquis of Dongcheng at ten thousand households;

sentences.zh: 封敖爲開陵侯；

translations.literal: he enfeoffed Ao as Marquis of Kailing;
translations.idiomatic: Ao took Marquis of Kailing;

sentences.zh: 封陽爲卯石侯，橫海將軍說爲按道侯，橫海校尉福爲繚嫈侯。

translations.literal: he enfeoffed Yang as Marquis of Maoshi; Across-the-Sea General Yue became Marquis of Andao; Across-the-Sea Colonel Fu became Marquis of Liaoying.
translations.idiomatic: Wu Yang became Marquis of Maoshi; Han Yue Marquis of Andao; Colonel Fu Marquis of Liaoying.

sentences.zh: 福者，城陽王子，故爲海常侯，坐法失爵，從軍亡功，以宗室故侯。

translations.literal: Fu was a son of Prince of Chengyang; formerly had been Marquis of Haichang; by law offense lost noble rank; following the army without merit—because of imperial-house kinship was made marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Fu was a Chengyang prince’s son, stripped of Haichang for a crime; he earned Liaoying on lineage despite thin battlefield credit.

sentences.zh: 及東粵將多軍，漢兵至，棄軍降，封爲無錫侯。

translations.literal: Moreover Eastern Yue general Duo Jun—when Han troops arrived abandoned army and surrendered—was enfeoffed Marquis of Wuxi.
translations.idiomatic: General Duo Jun threw down his command at Han’s approach and won Marquis of Wuxi.

sentences.zh: 故甌駱將左黃同斬西于王，封爲下鄜侯。

translations.literal: Former Ou-Luo general Zuo Huangtong beheaded King Xiyu and was enfeoffed Marquis of Xiafu.
translations.idiomatic: Ou-Luo’s Zuo Huangtong slew King Xiyu and took Marquis of Xiafu.

sentences.zh: 於是天子曰「東粵狹多阻，閩粵悍，數反復」，詔軍吏皆將其民徙處江、淮之間。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven said ‘Eastern Yue is narrow with many barriers; Minyue is fierce and repeatedly turns back,’ ordering military clerks all to lead their people relocated to dwell between Yangzi and Huai.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged Eastern Yue cramped and defensible, Minyue restive—he ordered the population resettled between the Yangzi and Huai.

sentences.zh: 東粵地遂虛。

translations.literal: Eastern Yue territory thereupon lay empty.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Yue became an empty march.

sentences.zh: 朝鮮王滿，燕人。

translations.literal: King Man of Chosun was a Yan man.
translations.idiomatic: Chosun’s King Man came from Yan.

sentences.zh: 自始燕時，嘗略屬真番、朝鮮，爲置吏築障。

translations.literal: From Yan’s beginning era, they once seized and attached Zhenfan and Chosun, for them placing officials and building barriers.
translations.idiomatic: Early Yan had annexed Zhenfan and Chosun—posting magistrates and frontier walls.

sentences.zh: 秦滅燕，屬遼東外徼。

translations.literal: When Qin destroyed Yan, they belonged to Liaodong’s outer marches.
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s conquest of Yan folded them into Liaodong’s outer belt.

sentences.zh: 漢興，爲遠難守，復修遼東故塞，至浿水爲界，屬燕。

translations.literal: When Han rose, because distant and hard to guard, they restored Liaodong’s old barriers, taking as boundary to the Pe River, belonging to Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Han deemed them too distant to hold—rebuilt old Liaodong walls with the Pe as boundary under Yan.

sentences.zh: 燕王盧綰反，入匈奴，滿亡命，聚党千餘人，椎結蠻夷服而東走出塞，渡浿水，居秦故空地上下障，稍伇屬真番、朝鮮蠻夷及故燕、齊亡在者王之，都王險。

translations.literal: Prince of Yan Lu Wan rebelled and entered the Xiongnu; Man fled his life, gathered a faction more than a thousand, wore topknot barbarian Yi dress and fled east out of the barrier, crossed the Pe River, dwelt in Qin’s former empty lands above and below the barriers, gradually bringing Zhenfan and Chosun barbarian Yi and former Yan and Qi fugitives there under dependent rule as king, capital at Wangxian.
translations.idiomatic: When Lu Wan fled to the steppe, Man led a thousand refugees east across the Pe into abandoned Qin fort zones—absorbed local tribes and Yan–Qi exiles—and founded Chosun at Wangxian.

sentences.zh: 會孝惠、高後天下初定，遼東太守即約滿爲外臣，保塞外蠻夷，毋使盜邊；

translations.literal: It happened when Emperor Xiaohui and Empress Gao had newly settled All-under-Heaven; Liaodong’s grand administrator then covenanted with Man as outer vassal, guarding barbarian Yi beyond the barrier, not letting them raid the border;
translations.idiomatic: Once Hui-di and Empress Lü had stabilized the realm, Liaodong’s governor recognized Man as outer vassal—keep tribal raiders off Han soil;

sentences.zh: 蠻夷君長欲入見天子，勿得禁止。

translations.literal: if barbarian Yi chiefs wished to enter and see the Son of Heaven, they must not be forbidden.
translations.idiomatic: and let tribal chiefs travel to court if they wished.

sentences.zh: 以聞，上許之，以故滿得以兵威財物侵降其旁小邑，真番、臨屯皆來服屬，方數千里。

translations.literal: Reported upward; the emperor approved it; for this reason Man was able by military might and goods to encroach and bring to surrender neighboring small settlements; Zhenfan and Lintun both came to submit in dependency—a territory several thousand li square.
translations.idiomatic: The throne agreed—Man used pressure and gold to absorb neighboring towns until Zhenfan and Lintun acknowledged him across thousands of square li.

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫右渠，所誘漢亡人滋多，又未嘗入見；

translations.literal: Passed to son down to grandson Youqu; those Han fugitives he enticed grew ever more numerous; moreover he never entered court audience;
translations.idiomatic: Through son to grandson Youqu—he sheltered ever more Han fugitives and never came to court;

sentences.zh: 真番、辰國欲上書見天子，又雍閼弗通。

translations.literal: Zhenfan and Chen state wished to submit memorials and see the Son of Heaven; again they were blocked and obstructed without passage.
translations.idiomatic: Zhenfan and Chen wished to petition the throne—Youqu choked every channel.

sentences.zh: 元封二年，漢使涉何譙諭右渠，終不肯奉詔。

translations.literal: Yuanfeng second year, Han envoy She He rebukingly instructed Youqu; to the end he would not obey edict.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanfeng 2 She He lectured Youqu—who still defied the decree.

sentences.zh: 何去至界，臨浿水，使馭刺殺送何者朝鮮裨王長，即渡水，馳入塞，遂歸報天子曰「殺朝鮮將」。

translations.literal: When He reached the border, overlooking the Pe River, he ordered his charioteer to stab and kill Chosun secondary king Chang who was seeing He off; immediately crossing the water, he galloped into the barrier, thereupon returned reporting to the Son of Heaven saying ‘killed a Chosun general.’
translations.idiomatic: At the Pe, He had his driver murder escorting secondary king Chang, bolted back across the river, and claimed a general slain.

sentences.zh: 上爲其名美，弗詰，拜何爲遼東東部都尉。

translations.literal: The emperor for its name sounding fine did not investigate; appointed He as eastern-section commandant of Liaodong.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor liked the headline and skipped inquiry—made She He eastern Liaodong commandant.

sentences.zh: 朝鮮怨何，發兵襲攻，殺何。

translations.literal: Chosun resented He, raised troops in sneak attack, and killed He.
translations.idiomatic: Chosun avenged itself on She He with a raid.

sentences.zh: 天子募罪人擊朝鮮。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven recruited convicts to strike Chosun.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor raised a convict army against Chosun.

sentences.zh: 其秋，遣樓船將軍楊僕從齊浮勃海，兵五萬，左將軍荀彘出遼東，誅右渠。

translations.literal: That autumn he dispatched Towered-Ship General Yang Pu from Qi floating Bohai, troops fifty thousand; Left General Xun Zhi exited Liaodong—to punish Youqu.
translations.idiomatic: That fall Yang Pu sailed fifty thousand from Qi across Bohai while Xun Zhi marched from Liaodong—both aimed at Youqu.

sentences.zh: 右渠發兵距險。

translations.literal: Youqu raised troops to bar the narrows.
translations.idiomatic: Youqu held the defiles.

sentences.zh: 左將軍卒多率遼東士兵先縱，敗散。

translations.literal: Left General’s corps commander Duo led Liaodong soldiers in rash advance first; they were defeated and scattered.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi’s corps commander Duo threw Liaodong levies forward too soon—they shattered.

sentences.zh: 多還走，坐法斬。

translations.literal: Duo fled back; by law he was beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Duo ran and died on the execution ground.

sentences.zh: 樓船將齊兵七千人先至王險。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship General’s Qi troops seven thousand first arrived at Wangxian.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu’s seven thousand Qi men reached Wangxian first.

sentences.zh: 右渠城守，窺知樓船軍少，即出擊樓船，樓船軍敗走。

translations.literal: Youqu defended by walls; spying that Towered-Ship army was few, immediately exited striking Towered-Ship; Towered-Ship army fled in defeat.
translations.idiomatic: Youqu saw Yang Pu’s thin ranks and sallied—Pu broke.

sentences.zh: 將軍僕失其衆，遁山中十餘日，稍求收散卒，復聚。

translations.literal: General Pu lost his host, hid in mountains more than ten days, gradually sought gathering scattered soldiers, and regathered.
translations.idiomatic: Pu lost his command and hid two weeks before scraping survivors back together.

sentences.zh: 左將軍擊朝鮮浿水西軍，未能破。

translations.literal: Left General struck Chosun’s army west of the Pe River; could not break them.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi failed to crack the force west of the Pe.

sentences.zh: 天子爲兩將未有利，乃使衛山因兵威往諭右渠。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven because neither general had gain, then sent Wei Shan leveraging military prestige to go instruct Youqu.
translations.idiomatic: With both wings stalled, the emperor sent Wei Shan under arms to parley.

sentences.zh: 右渠見使者，頓首謝：「願降，恐將詐殺臣；

translations.literal: Youqu saw the envoy, knocked his head apologizing: ‘I wish to surrender; I fear generals will treacherously kill your servant;’
translations.idiomatic: Youqu kowtowed: ‘I would yield—yet fear your generals mean treachery;’

sentences.zh: 今見信節，請服降。

translations.literal: now seeing trusted credentials, I request to submit in surrender.’
translations.idiomatic: these tallies convince me—I ask to come in.’

sentences.zh: 」遣太子入謝，獻馬五千匹，及饋軍糧。

translations.literal: ’ He dispatched crown prince to enter apology, presenting five thousand horses and provisioning army grain.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He sent his heir with five thousand horses and army grain.

sentences.zh: 人衆萬餘持兵，方度浿水，使者及左將軍疑其爲變，謂太子已服降，宜令人毋持兵，太子亦疑使者左將軍詐之，遂不度浿水，復引歸。

translations.literal: More than ten thousand people bearing arms were just crossing the Pe River; envoy and Left General suspected them of plotting mutiny—said crown prince having submitted in surrender ought order men not bear arms; crown prince also suspected envoy and Left General would deceive him; thereupon did not cross the Pe River, drew back and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Ten thousand armed escorts reached the Pe—Han demanded they disarm; the prince feared a trap—turned back without crossing.

sentences.zh: 山報，天子誅山。

translations.literal: Shan reported; the Son of Heaven executed Shan.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Shan’s report bought him execution.

sentences.zh: 左將軍破浿水上軍乃前至城下，圍其西北。

translations.literal: Left General broke Chosun’s army on the Pe River then advanced to below the walls, besieging its northwest.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi smashed the force on the Pe and invested Wangxian’s northwest.

sentences.zh: 樓船亦往會，居城南。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship also went to join, occupying south face.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu linked up on the south face.

sentences.zh: 右渠遂堅城守，數月未能下。

translations.literal: Youqu thereupon firmly defended by walls; several months could not take them.
translations.idiomatic: Youqu held tight—for months neither wing broke in.

sentences.zh: 左將軍素侍中，幸，將燕，代卒，悍，乘勝，軍多驕。

translations.literal: Left General had long attended within the palace, favored; leading Yan and Dai troops, fierce, riding victory—the army was largely arrogant.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi was an imperial favorite with swaggering Yan–Dai veterans flush with confidence.

sentences.zh: 樓船將齊卒，入海已多敗亡，其先與右渠戰，困辱亡卒，卒皆恐，將心慚，其圍右渠，常持和節。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship led Qi soldiers; entering the sea already many defeated and lost; earlier fighting Youqu, distressed and humiliated lost soldiers—all soldiers were fearful; the general’s heart ashamed—in besieging Youqu he constantly held peace credentials.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu’s Qi troops had bled crossing the sea; first clash shamed them—the general favored talk over assault.

sentences.zh: 左將軍急擊之，朝鮮大臣乃陰間使人私約降樓船，往來言，尚未肯決。

translations.literal: Left General urgently struck them; Chosun great ministers then secretly sent men privately covenanting surrender to Towered-Ship—messages went back and forth; still not willing to decide.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi hammered the walls while Korean ministers secretly sounded out Yang Pu—no deal closed.

sentences.zh: 左將軍數與樓船期戰，樓船欲就其約，不會。

translations.literal: Left General repeatedly set battle rendezvous with Towered-Ship; Towered-Ship wished to pursue his covenant and did not join.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi scheduled joint attacks—Yang Pu skipped every meeting to nurse his surrender talks.

sentences.zh: 左將軍亦使人求間隙降下朝鮮，不肯，心附樓船。

translations.literal: Left General also sent men seeking openings to bring Chosun down in surrender; they refused—their hearts attached to Towered-Ship.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi’s own envoys got nowhere—Korean nobles trusted only Yang Pu.

sentences.zh: 以故兩將不相得。

translations.literal: For this reason the two generals did not get along.
translations.idiomatic: The two commanders despised each other.

sentences.zh: 左將軍心意樓船前有失軍罪，今與朝鮮和善而又不降，疑其有反計，未敢發。

translations.literal: Left General reckoned Towered-Ship earlier had offense of losing army; now being peacefully friendly with Chosun yet again not surrendering—suspected he harbored rebel plot—did not dare expose it.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi suspected Yang Pu—already stained by defeat—of cutting a separate deal but lacked proof to strike.

sentences.zh: 天子曰：「將率不能前，乃使衛山諭降右渠，不能顓決，與左將軍相誤，卒沮約。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven said: ‘Generals could not advance; then sent Wei Shan to instruct surrender from Youqu—could not decide exclusively, misled Left General, ultimately thwarted covenant.’
translations.idiomatic: The emperor fumed: ‘Generals stalled; Wei Shan botched Youqu’s surrender and wrecked the armistice.’

sentences.zh: 今兩將圍城又乖異，以故久不決。

translations.literal: Now two generals besieging the city again diverge—for this reason long unresolved.’
translations.idiomatic: Now both besiege the capital yet refuse to cooperate—that is why it holds.’

sentences.zh: 」使故濟南太守公孫遂往正之，有便宜得以從事。

translations.literal: ’ He dispatched former Grand Administrator of Jinan Gongsun Sui to go straighten them—with expedient authority to act as needed.
translations.idiomatic: ’ He sent ex-governor Gongsun Sui of Jinan with discretionary powers.

sentences.zh: 遂至，左將軍曰：「朝鮮當下久矣，不下者，樓船數期不會。

translations.literal: When Sui arrived, Left General said: ‘Chosun ought to have been taken long ago; what prevents taking it is Towered-Ship repeatedly missing rendezvous.’
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi told Sui: ‘This city should have fallen—Yang Pu keeps missing joint strikes.’

sentences.zh: 」具以素所意告遂曰：「今如此不取，恐爲大害，非獨樓船，又且與朝鮮共滅吾軍。

translations.literal: ’ He fully told what he had long suspected to Sui saying: ‘Now if thus we do not seize him, I fear great harm—not Towered-Ship alone but moreover together with Chosun extinguishing our army.’
translations.idiomatic: ‘Act now or Yang Pu may hand our army to the enemy.’

sentences.zh: 」遂亦以爲然，而以節召樓船將軍入左將軍軍計事，即令左將軍戲下執縛樓船將軍，並其軍。

translations.literal: ’ Sui likewise deemed it so; bearing credentials he summoned Towered-Ship General into Left General’s army to consult affairs—immediately ordered Left General’s headquarters guards seize and bind Towered-Ship General and merge his army.
translations.idiomatic: Sui agreed—summoned Yang Pu under imperial tally—then had Xun Zhi’s guards arrest him and absorb his corps.

sentences.zh: 以報，天子誅遂。

translations.literal: Reporting upward, the Son of Heaven executed Sui.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor executed Gongsun Sui for overreach.

sentences.zh: 左將軍已並兩軍，即急擊朝鮮。

translations.literal: Left General having already merged both armies, immediately urgently struck Chosun.
translations.idiomatic: With Yang Pu’s troops folded in, Xun Zhi struck hard.

sentences.zh: 朝鮮相路人、相韓陶、尼谿相參、將軍王夾相與謀曰：「始欲降樓船，樓船今執，獨左將軍並將，戰益急，恐不能與，王又不肯降。

translations.literal: Chosun ministers Lu Ren, Han Tao, minister Shen of Nixi, General Wang Jia consulted together saying: ‘At first we wished to surrender to Towered-Ship; Towered-Ship is now seized—Left General alone commands merged forces; battle grows ever more urgent—we fear we cannot withstand; the king again refuses surrender.’
translations.idiomatic: Ministers Lu Ren, Han Tao, Shen of Nixi, and Wang Jia plotted: ‘We meant to yield to Yang Pu—Pu is chained—only Xun Zhi drives the assault; the king still won’t bend.’

sentences.zh: 」陶、唊、路人皆亡降漢。

translations.literal: ’ Tao, Jia, and Lu Ren all fled surrendering to Han.
translations.idiomatic: ’ Tao, Jia, and Lu Ren defected to Han.

sentences.zh: 路人道死。

translations.literal: Lu Ren died on the road.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Ren died en route.

sentences.zh: 元封三年夏，尼谿相參乃使人殺朝鮮王右渠來降。

translations.literal: Yuanfeng third year summer, minister Shen of Nixi then sent men killing King of Chosun Youqu and came to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanfeng 3 summer Shen of Nixi had Youqu murdered and surrendered.

sentences.zh: 王險城未下，故右渠之大臣成已又反，復攻吏。

translations.literal: Wangxian city not yet taken; former Youqu’s great minister Cheng Yi again rebelled, again attacking clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Wangxian still held—minister Cheng Yi revolted anew against Han officials.

sentences.zh: 左將軍使右渠子長、降相路人子最，告諭其民，誅成已。

translations.literal: Left General ordered Youqu’s son Chang and surrendered minister Lu Ren’s son Zui to announce instructing the people and executed Cheng Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Zhi sent Youqu’s heir Chang and Lu Ren’s son Zui to rally the city—then executed Cheng Yi.

sentences.zh: 故遂定朝鮮爲真番、臨屯、樂浪、玄菟四郡。

translations.literal: Therefore thereupon settled Chosun as Zhenfan, Lintun, Lelang, and Xuantu—four commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Chosun became four commanderies: Zhenfan, Lintun, Lelang, and Xuantu.

sentences.zh: 封參爲澅清侯，陶爲秋苴侯，唊爲平州侯，長爲幾侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed Shen as Marquis of Huqing, Tao as Marquis of Qiuju, Jia as Marquis of Pingzhou, Chang as Marquis of Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Shen took Huqing, Tao Qiuju, Jia Pingzhou, Chang Ji.

sentences.zh: 最以父死頗有功，爲沮陽侯。

translations.literal: Zui because father died yet had considerable merit became Marquis of Juyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zui earned Juyang on his father’s service.

sentences.zh: 左將軍征至，坐爭功相嫉乖計，棄市。

translations.literal: Left General summoned to arrive—sat convicted for wrangling merit, mutual jealousy, perverse planning—public execution in marketplace.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled to Chang’an, Xun Zhi died in the marketplace for jealous intrigue.

sentences.zh: 樓船將軍亦坐兵至列口當待左將軍，擅先縱，失亡多，當誅，贖爲庶人。

translations.literal: Towered-Ship General also sat convicted that troops reaching Liekou ought to have awaited Left General—rashly advancing first—many losses—liable for execution—ransomed to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Pu should have waited at Liekou—his rash advance cost lives—capital sentence commuted to ransom and commoner status.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：楚、粵之先，曆世有土。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Chu and Yue’s ancestors through generations held territory.
translations.idiomatic: Appraisal: Chu and Yue held soil for generations.

sentences.zh: 及周之衰，楚地方五千里，而勾踐亦以粵伯。

translations.literal: Down to Zhou’s decline, Chu’s territory measured five thousand li, and Goujian also by Yue became hegemon.
translations.idiomatic: Late Zhou saw Chu span five thousand li while Goujian lorded Yue.

sentences.zh: 秦滅諸侯，唯楚尚有滇王。

translations.literal: When Qin destroyed the feudal lords, only Chu still had the King of Dian.
translations.idiomatic: Qin abolished the feudal order—yet Dian’s kings endured under Chu’s shadow.

sentences.zh: 漢誅西南夷，獨滇復寵。

translations.literal: Han punished the southwestern Yi; only Dian again received favor.
translations.idiomatic: Han crushed the southwestern Yi—only Dian won renewed favor.

sentences.zh: 及東粵滅國遷衆，繇王居股等猶爲萬戶侯。

translations.literal: Down to Eastern Yue destroying state and relocating the masses, King Yao Jugu and others still became ten-thousand-household marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Even after Eastern Yue fell and its people were moved, men like Yao Jugu kept ten-thousand-household marquisates.

sentences.zh: 三方之開，皆自好事之臣。

translations.literal: The opening of the three directions all stemmed from ministers fond of stirring affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Expansion on three frontiers began with ambitious courtiers.

sentences.zh: 故西南夷發于唐蒙、司馬相如，兩粵起嚴助、硃買臣，朝鮮由涉何。

translations.literal: Therefore southwestern Yi arose from Tang Meng and Sima Xiangru; the two Yues rose from Yan Zhu and Zhu Maichen; Chosun came through She He.
translations.idiomatic: The southwest opened with Tang Meng and Sima Xiangru; the two Yues with Yan Zhu and Zhu Maichen; Chosun with She He.

sentences.zh: 遭世富盛，動能成功，然已勤矣。

translations.literal: Encountering an age rich and flourishing, movements could achieve success—yet already it was toilsome.
translations.idiomatic: An affluent age made conquest possible—but the effort was severe.

sentences.zh: 追觀太宗填撫尉佗，豈古所謂「招攜以禮，懷遠以德」者哉！

translations.literal: Looking back on Emperor Taizong’s settlement and soothing of Commandant Tuo—is this not what antiquity called ‘drawing the wavering with ritual, embracing the distant with virtue’!
translations.idiomatic: Compare Emperor Wen’s gentle handling of Zhao Tuo—the very model of winning allies by ritual and distant peoples by virtue!
